title: Driver Accessing Invalid Memory at Elevated IRQL
shortDescription: A kernel-mode driver attempted to access pageable memory at a process IRQL that was too high.
fullDescription: This stop code indicates that a kernel-mode process or driver attempted to access a memory address to which it did not have permission at an interrupt request level (IRQL) that was too high. The most common root cause is a faulty or incompatible device driver that references an incorrect memory address. It can also occur due to defective RAM, overclocking instability, or corrupted system files.

causes.cause: Antivirus or security software kernel conflict

symptoms: Blue screen appears with stop code IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL
System crashes randomly during normal use or gaming
Crash occurs shortly after installing a new driver or hardware
System restarts unexpectedly without warning
Minidump files generated in C:\Windows\Minidump

fixSteps.title: Boot into Safe Mode
fixSteps.instruction: Restart the PC and press F8 or hold Shift while clicking Restart. Navigate to Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > Startup Settings and select Safe Mode with Networking.

fixSteps.title: Update or roll back drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager, expand each category, right-click recently updated devices and select Update Driver or Roll Back Driver. Focus on network, display, and storage controllers.

fixSteps.title: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: Press Win+R, type mdsched.exe, and select Restart now and check for problems. Review results in Event Viewer under Windows Logs > System after reboot.

fixSteps.title: Run System File Checker
fixSteps.instruction: Open an elevated Command Prompt and run: sfc /scannow. If issues are found, follow up with: DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

fixSteps.title: Remove overclocking settings
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS/UEFI and reset all overclocking values to defaults including CPU multiplier, RAM timings, and voltage settings.

whenToCallPro: If the BSOD persists after driver updates and memory diagnostics pass, a professional can analyze minidump files to pinpoint the exact faulting driver or hardware component.

faq.q: Can bad RAM cause IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL?
faq.a: Yes. Faulty RAM is one of the most common hardware causes. Run Windows Memory Diagnostic or MemTest86 to check your memory modules.

faq.q: Does this error always mean a driver problem?
faq.a: Not always, but driver issues are the most frequent cause. Check Device Manager for devices with warning icons and review recently installed or updated drivers.

faq.q: Can I fix this without reinstalling Windows?
faq.a: In most cases, yes. Updating drivers, running SFC, and testing RAM usually resolves the issue without a full reinstall.

title: Critical System Process Terminated Unexpectedly
shortDescription: A critical Windows system process terminated unexpectedly, forcing the system to halt.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a process essential to Windows operation stops unexpectedly. Critical processes include csrss.exe, wininit.exe, smss.exe, and others that Windows cannot run without. Common triggers include corrupted system files, bad Windows updates, driver conflicts, or malware infection.
symptoms: Blue screen with CRITICAL_PROCESS_DIED message
Windows fails to boot and enters automatic repair loop
System crashes during startup or shortly after login
Repeated BSOD after Windows Update
Event Viewer shows critical process termination events

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Boot into Safe Mode or Recovery. Open elevated Command Prompt and run: sfc /scannow followed by DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

fixSteps.title: Uninstall recent Windows updates
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Update & Security > View update history > Uninstall updates. Remove the most recent quality or feature update.

fixSteps.title: Run a full malware scan
fixSteps.instruction: Boot into Safe Mode and run a full system scan using Windows Defender or a reputable antimalware tool.

fixSteps.title: Check disk health
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt and run: chkdsk C: /f /r. Also check S.M.A.R.T. status using CrystalDiskInfo or similar utility.

fixSteps.title: Perform System Restore
fixSteps.instruction: Boot into Advanced Startup Options > Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > System Restore. Choose a restore point from before the issue started.

whenToCallPro: If System Restore and SFC/DISM fail to resolve the issue, or if the system cannot boot into Safe Mode, professional assistance may be needed to repair or reinstall Windows.

faq.q: Is CRITICAL_PROCESS_DIED caused by viruses?
faq.a: It can be. Malware that corrupts or interferes with essential system processes like csrss.exe can trigger this BSOD. Running a full malware scan is recommended.

faq.q: Can a Windows update cause this error?
faq.a: Yes. A failed or incompatible Windows update is one of the most common triggers. Uninstalling the recent update often resolves the problem.

faq.q: Will I lose my files?
faq.a: Usually not. This error typically affects system files rather than personal data. However, backing up important files is always recommended before attempting repairs.

title: Exception in System Service Handler
shortDescription: An exception occurred while executing a routine that transitions from non-privileged to privileged code.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a system service call handler encounters an unhandled exception. It commonly indicates a driver bug, corrupted system files, or incompatible software. The error often names the specific driver file responsible in the BSOD parameters.

causes.cause: Buggy or outdated device driver (often GPU drivers)
Insufficient disk space on system drive

symptoms: Blue screen displays SYSTEM_SERVICE_EXCEPTION with a driver file name
Crash occurs during graphics-intensive tasks or gaming
System freezes then shows BSOD after installing new software
Repeated crashes with different driver names cited

fixSteps.title: Identify the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Check the BSOD screen for the driver file name (e.g., win32kfull.sys, ntfs.sys). Use BlueScreenView to analyze minidump files in C:\Windows\Minidump.

fixSteps.title: Update the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Download the latest driver from the hardware manufacturer's website. Avoid using Windows Update for GPU drivers; use NVIDIA, AMD, or Intel driver packages directly.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow then DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

fixSteps.title: Uninstall conflicting software
fixSteps.instruction: Remove recently installed applications, especially antivirus or system utilities, from Settings > Apps > Apps & features.

whenToCallPro: If the specific faulting driver cannot be identified or if the crash persists after driver updates and system file repairs.

faq.q: What does the driver file name in the error mean?
faq.a: It identifies the specific driver that caused the crash. For example, nvlddmkm.sys is NVIDIA's display driver, while ntfs.sys is the NTFS file system driver.

faq.q: Can antivirus software cause this BSOD?
faq.a: Yes. Some antivirus kernel-mode drivers conflict with Windows system services. Try temporarily disabling or uninstalling the antivirus to test.

faq.q: Is this error caused by hardware failure?
faq.a: Rarely. It is almost always a software or driver issue. Hardware testing should only be pursued if all software fixes fail.

title: Kernel Data Could Not Be Read Into Memory
shortDescription: The requested page of kernel data could not be read from the paging file into memory.
fullDescription: This BSOD indicates that Windows attempted to read kernel data from the paging file (virtual memory) on disk, but the read operation failed. This is commonly caused by bad sectors on the hard drive, a failing storage device, faulty RAM, corrupt page file, or loose SATA/power cables.

causes.cause: Bad sectors or failing hard drive/SSD

symptoms: Blue screen with KERNEL_DATA_INPAGE_ERROR
System becomes extremely slow before crashing
Hard drive makes clicking or grinding noises
Files become corrupted or disappear unexpectedly
Frequent disk-related errors in Event Viewer

fixSteps.title: Check disk health with CHKDSK
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt and run: chkdsk C: /f /r /x. This scans for and attempts to repair bad sectors. The system will reboot to complete the scan.

fixSteps.title: Test RAM with Memory Diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: Press Win+R, type mdsched.exe, choose Restart now. For thorough testing, use MemTest86 from a bootable USB.

fixSteps.title: Reset the paging file
fixSteps.instruction: Go to System Properties > Advanced > Performance Settings > Advanced > Virtual Memory > Change. Uncheck automatic management, set to No paging file, restart, then re-enable automatic management.

fixSteps.title: Check physical cable connections
fixSteps.instruction: Power off the PC, open the case, and reseat SATA data and power cables to the hard drive/SSD. Replace cables if they appear damaged.

fixSteps.title: Check S.M.A.R.T. disk status
fixSteps.instruction: Download and run CrystalDiskInfo. Check the Health Status for your drives. Any status other than Good indicates the drive should be replaced.

whenToCallPro: If S.M.A.R.T. shows drive failure or if CHKDSK finds unfixable bad sectors, you likely need a drive replacement and data recovery services.

faq.q: Does this mean my hard drive is failing?
faq.a: It often does, especially if accompanied by slow performance and unusual drive noises. Check S.M.A.R.T. status immediately and back up your data.

faq.q: Can this error be caused by RAM?
faq.a: Yes. If the paging file data in RAM becomes corrupted due to faulty memory, this error can occur. Run Windows Memory Diagnostic to test.

faq.q: Should I replace the hard drive immediately?
faq.a: If CrystalDiskInfo shows a Caution or Bad health status, replace the drive as soon as possible. Back up all important data first.

title: Invalid Memory Reference in Non-Paged Pool
shortDescription: The system referenced memory in the non-paged pool that was invalid, indicating a driver or hardware issue.
fullDescription: This error occurs when the system attempts to access a portion of memory that should always be resident (non-paged) but finds it is not available. This typically points to faulty RAM, a buggy driver, or corrupted system files. It can also be triggered by incompatible software or a failing storage device.

causes.cause: Corrupted NTFS volume or system files

symptoms: Blue screen with PAGE_FAULT_IN_NONPAGED_AREA
BSOD occurs when launching specific applications
System crashes under heavy memory usage
Crash occurs after adding new RAM modules

fixSteps.title: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: Press Win+R, type mdsched.exe, choose Restart now. After reboot, check Event Viewer > Windows Logs > System for MemoryDiagnostics-Results.

fixSteps.title: Update device drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager and update all drivers with yellow warning icons. Focus on storage, network, and display adapters.

fixSteps.title: Check and repair system files
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt and run: sfc /scannow followed by chkdsk C: /f /r

fixSteps.title: Disable recently installed software
fixSteps.instruction: Use Clean Boot (msconfig > Services > Hide all Microsoft services > Disable all, then Startup tab > Open Task Manager > disable all startup items) to isolate the conflicting application.

whenToCallPro: If memory diagnostics find errors or if the BSOD persists after trying all software fixes, professional hardware diagnosis is recommended.

faq.q: What is the non-paged pool?
faq.a: The non-paged pool is an area of system memory (RAM) that is guaranteed to always stay in physical memory and never be paged to disk. Kernel-mode drivers use it for critical data.

faq.q: Can this be caused by new RAM?
faq.a: Yes. Incompatible RAM, improperly seated modules, or mismatched speeds can cause this error. Try removing newly added RAM to test.

faq.q: Does this always indicate a hardware problem?
faq.a: No. While RAM issues are common, this BSOD is frequently caused by buggy drivers or corrupted system files, which can be fixed with software repairs.

title: Deferred Procedure Call Watchdog Timeout
shortDescription: A DPC routine took longer than expected, causing the watchdog timer to trigger a bugcheck.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a Deferred Procedure Call (DPC) routine runs for longer than the system's watchdog timer allows. This usually indicates a driver that is taking too long to complete an operation, often related to storage controllers (especially SSD firmware issues), network adapters, or display drivers.

causes.cause: Outdated or incompatible SSD/storage controller driver
SSD firmware issue (especially with AHCI/IDE mode mismatch)
Outdated IRST (Intel Rapid Storage Technology) driver

symptoms: Blue screen with DPC_WATCHDOG_VIOLATION
System freezes for several seconds before BSOD
Crash occurs frequently with SSDs
BSOD happens during disk-intensive operations

fixSteps.title: Update storage controller drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Download the latest AHCI/NVMe driver from your SSD or motherboard manufacturer. For Intel systems, install the latest Intel RST driver from Intel's support site.

fixSteps.title: Update SSD firmware
fixSteps.instruction: Check your SSD manufacturer's website for firmware updates. Use their utility (Samsung Magician, Crucial Storage Executive, etc.) to update.

fixSteps.title: Check SATA mode in BIOS
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS/UEFI and ensure SATA mode is set to AHCI (not IDE). Warning: changing this on an existing installation may prevent booting without a registry modification first.

fixSteps.title: Run System File Checker
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt and run: sfc /scannow

whenToCallPro: If the issue persists after driver and firmware updates, or if changing SATA mode is required on an existing installation.

faq.q: Why does this mainly affect SSDs?
faq.a: SSDs require specific AHCI drivers to function properly. Using generic Microsoft drivers or IDE mode can cause the storage controller to hang, triggering the watchdog timeout.

faq.q: What is a DPC?
faq.a: A Deferred Procedure Call is a Windows mechanism that allows high-priority tasks to defer less critical work for later execution. If this deferred work takes too long, the watchdog timer triggers a BSOD.

faq.q: Can I fix this without updating BIOS?
faq.a: Usually yes. Updating the storage controller driver alone resolves this in most cases. BIOS updates are only needed if driver updates fail to fix the issue.

title: Severe Memory Management Error
shortDescription: Windows detected a severe memory management error, typically indicating faulty RAM or driver issues.
fullDescription: This BSOD indicates that the Windows Memory Manager encountered a critical error. The root cause is most commonly faulty RAM, but it can also be triggered by disk issues, outdated drivers, or corrupted system files. Since memory management is fundamental to Windows operation, this error can appear seemingly at random.

causes.cause: Corrupted system files or disk errors

symptoms: Blue screen with MEMORY_MANAGEMENT stop code
Random crashes during both idle and active use
Applications crash with out-of-memory errors
System performance degrades over time before crash

fixSteps.title: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: Press Win+R, type mdsched.exe, choose Restart now. For more thorough testing, download MemTest86 and run it from a bootable USB for several passes.

fixSteps.title: Update all device drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager and check for outdated drivers. Download latest versions from manufacturer websites, focusing on chipset, storage, and GPU drivers.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow then DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

fixSteps.title: Check for disk errors
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt and run: chkdsk C: /f /r

whenToCallPro: If MemTest86 confirms RAM errors, you will need to replace the faulty module(s). A professional can help identify exactly which module is failing in a multi-module configuration.

faq.q: Does MEMORY_MANAGEMENT mean my RAM is bad?
faq.a: Not necessarily. While faulty RAM is the most common cause, this error can also result from driver bugs, disk corruption, or software conflicts. Run Memory Diagnostic to confirm.

faq.q: Can overclocked RAM cause this?
faq.a: Yes. RAM overclocking or XMP profiles that push memory beyond stable limits can cause this BSOD. Try resetting RAM to default speeds in BIOS.

faq.q: How do I know which RAM stick is bad?
faq.a: Remove all but one RAM stick and test. If the error disappears, add sticks back one at a time. MemTest86 can also identify which module fails.

title: Hardware Error Detected by WHEA
shortDescription: The Windows Hardware Error Architecture detected a fatal, uncorrectable hardware error.
fullDescription: WHEA (Windows Hardware Error Architecture) monitors hardware components for errors. When it detects an uncorrectable error, this BSOD is triggered. This is almost always a genuine hardware problem - most commonly CPU overheating, unstable CPU voltage, failing RAM, or a motherboard issue. Overclocking is a very frequent trigger.

causes.cause: CPU overheating due to inadequate cooling

symptoms: Blue screen with WHEA_UNCORRECTABLE_ERROR
Crash occurs under heavy CPU load (gaming, rendering)
System shuts down when CPU temperature is high
BSOD appears immediately after enabling overclocking
Event Viewer shows WHEA-Logger warnings

fixSteps.title: Check CPU temperatures
fixSteps.instruction: Download HWMonitor or Core Temp and monitor CPU temperatures. If temperatures exceed 90C under load, cooling is insufficient.

fixSteps.title: Remove all overclocking
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS/UEFI and load optimized defaults. Disable XMP, reset CPU multiplier, voltage, and base clock to stock values.

fixSteps.title: Clean and reseat CPU cooler
fixSteps.instruction: Power off, open case, remove CPU cooler, clean old thermal paste with isopropyl alcohol, apply new thermal paste, and reseat the cooler firmly.

fixSteps.title: Test RAM
fixSteps.instruction: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic or MemTest86. Test each stick individually in the primary slot.

fixSteps.title: Update BIOS/UEFI
fixSteps.instruction: Check your motherboard manufacturer's website for BIOS updates that address stability. Follow their exact flashing instructions.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after removing overclocks, reseating the cooler, and testing RAM, the CPU or motherboard may be failing and requires professional diagnosis.

faq.q: Is WHEA_UNCORRECTABLE_ERROR always a hardware problem?
faq.a: Almost always. Unlike most BSODs that can be driver-related, this error specifically indicates hardware reporting a fault. The most common software-related cause is aggressive overclocking.

faq.q: Can this be caused by a Windows update?
faq.a: Very rarely. While a BIOS or microcode update delivered through Windows Update could theoretically trigger it, the overwhelming majority of cases are genuine hardware issues.

faq.q: Is my CPU dying?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Check thermals first - overheating is the most common cause. Also try removing overclocks. A truly failing CPU is less common than thermal or voltage issues.

title: Driver Accessed Paged Memory at Elevated IRQL
shortDescription: A device driver attempted to access paged memory at a kernel IRQL that was too high.
fullDescription: This is the driver-specific variant of IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL. It always names the specific driver file responsible. The driver attempted to access memory that had been paged to disk while running at an IRQL where page faults are not allowed. This is always a driver bug.

causes.cause: Buggy or outdated network adapter driver
Driver incompatible with current Windows version

symptoms: Blue screen naming a specific .sys file (e.g., ndis.sys, tcpip.sys, netio.sys)
Crash occurs during network activity or file transfers
BSOD appears after waking from sleep or hibernate
System crashes when connecting to Wi-Fi

fixSteps.title: Identify the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: The BSOD message typically names the driver file. Note it down. Use BlueScreenView to analyze minidump files for more details.

fixSteps.title: Update the identified driver
fixSteps.instruction: Find the device that uses the faulting .sys file in Device Manager. Download the latest driver from the hardware manufacturer's website.

fixSteps.title: Uninstall and reinstall the driver
fixSteps.instruction: In Device Manager, right-click the device > Uninstall device > check Delete driver software. Reboot and let Windows reinstall or install the manufacturer's driver.

fixSteps.title: Run driver verifier
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt, run: verifier /standard /all. Reboot. This will stress-test drivers and identify the faulty one. Run verifier /reset when done.

whenToCallPro: If the faulting driver cannot be identified or if Driver Verifier reveals multiple failing drivers.

faq.q: How do I find which driver is causing the crash?
faq.a: The BSOD screen usually displays the driver filename. Additionally, check minidump files in C:\Windows\Minidump using the free BlueScreenView utility.

faq.q: What does .sys file mean?
faq.a: Files with the .sys extension are kernel-mode drivers. They run with high privileges in Windows and have direct hardware access, which is why bugs in them cause system crashes.

faq.q: Can I just delete the bad driver file?
faq.a: No, never manually delete .sys files. Instead, uninstall the driver through Device Manager and let Windows replace it with a compatible version.

title: Unhandled Exception in System Thread
shortDescription: A system thread generated an exception that the error handler could not catch.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a system thread throws an exception that is not caught by any error handler. The BSOD parameters usually identify the specific driver responsible. This is commonly caused by display drivers, especially after Windows updates that break driver compatibility.

causes.cause: Driver broken by recent Windows update

symptoms: Blue screen with SYSTEM_THREAD_EXCEPTION_NOT_HANDLED and a driver filename
Crash occurs during boot, preventing Windows from loading
BSOD appears after a Windows update
System crashes when loading graphical desktop

fixSteps.title: Boot into Safe Mode
fixSteps.instruction: Use Advanced Startup (hold Shift while clicking Restart) > Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > Startup Settings > Safe Mode.

fixSteps.title: Update or reinstall the display driver
fixSteps.instruction: In Safe Mode, use DDU (Display Driver Uninstaller) to completely remove the GPU driver. Reboot and install the latest driver from NVIDIA, AMD, or Intel.

fixSteps.title: Roll back Windows update
fixSteps.instruction: In Advanced Startup > Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > Uninstall Updates. Select Uninstall latest quality update or feature update.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC from recovery
fixSteps.instruction: In Advanced Startup > Command Prompt, run: sfc /scannow /offbootdir=C:\ /offwindir=C:\Windows

whenToCallPro: If Windows cannot boot even in Safe Mode, or if the error persists after driver reinstallation.

faq.q: Why does this happen after Windows Update?
faq.a: Windows updates sometimes include driver updates that are incompatible with your specific hardware. Rolling back the update and installing the manufacturer's driver usually fixes it.

faq.q: What if I cannot boot into Safe Mode?
faq.a: Use Windows Recovery Environment (boot from Windows installation media), go to Repair your computer > Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > Command Prompt to run repairs.

faq.q: Is this always a GPU driver issue?
faq.a: While GPU drivers are the most common cause, any driver can trigger this BSOD. The error message names the specific driver file responsible.

title: Kernel Mode Exception Not Handled
shortDescription: The Windows kernel encountered an exception that the error handler did not catch.
fullDescription: This BSOD indicates that a kernel-mode program generated an exception which the error handler did not catch. It is typically caused by driver bugs, memory conflicts, or hardware incompatibilities. The BSOD parameters often include the address of the faulting instruction and the exception code.
symptoms: Blue screen with KMODE_EXCEPTION_NOT_HANDLED
Crash occurs during hardware-intensive tasks
System becomes unresponsive before BSOD
Error appears after installing new hardware

fixSteps.title: Identify faulting driver from minidump
fixSteps.instruction: Use BlueScreenView to open minidump files in C:\Windows\Minidump. The highlighted driver in the stack trace is likely the cause.

fixSteps.title: Update or remove the driver
fixSteps.instruction: Download the latest version of the identified driver from the manufacturer. If unavailable, uninstall the driver and let Windows use a generic one.

fixSteps.title: Test RAM
fixSteps.instruction: Run MemTest86 from a bootable USB for at least 4 passes. Also try removing one RAM stick at a time to isolate faulty modules.

fixSteps.title: Update BIOS
fixSteps.instruction: Check your motherboard manufacturer's website for BIOS updates. Follow their specific update procedure carefully.

whenToCallPro: If minidump analysis does not reveal a clear culprit, or if the error persists after driver updates and RAM testing.

faq.q: What is kernel mode?
faq.a: Kernel mode is the most privileged execution mode in Windows, where the OS core and drivers run. Errors here crash the entire system because there is no higher-level handler to catch them.

faq.q: Can this error corrupt my data?
faq.a: The BSOD itself does not corrupt data, but the underlying cause (like disk driver issues) might. Always maintain backups.

faq.q: Is this the same as SYSTEM_THREAD_EXCEPTION_NOT_HANDLED?
faq.a: They are similar but technically different. KMODE applies to kernel-mode exceptions generally, while SYSTEM_THREAD is specific to system thread exceptions. Troubleshooting steps are largely the same.

title: Unexpected Kernel Mode Trap
shortDescription: The CPU generated a trap that the kernel did not expect or could not handle.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when the CPU generates a trap (such as a bound trap, double fault, or invalid opcode) that the kernel failed to catch. The most common variant is a double fault (trap 0x00000008), which occurs when the kernel stack overflows. It is frequently caused by hardware failure, overheating, or severe driver bugs.

causes.cause: Kernel stack overflow due to buggy driver

symptoms: Blue screen with UNEXPECTED_KERNEL_MODE_TRAP
System crashes under CPU stress or heat
Crash occurs randomly without apparent trigger
BSOD after adding new hardware or drivers

fixSteps.title: Check CPU temperature
fixSteps.instruction: Install HWMonitor and check CPU temperatures. If idle temps exceed 60C or load temps exceed 90C, the cooling system needs attention.

fixSteps.title: Run memory diagnostics
fixSteps.instruction: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic and MemTest86 to check for RAM errors.

fixSteps.title: Update drivers and Windows
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure all drivers are up to date, particularly chipset and storage drivers. Install all pending Windows updates.

fixSteps.title: Remove overclock and test stability
fixSteps.instruction: Reset BIOS to defaults and test system stability. If the error stops, the overclock was exceeding hardware limits.

whenToCallPro: If temperatures are normal, RAM tests pass, and the error persists with stock settings, the CPU or motherboard may be failing.

faq.q: What is a double fault?
faq.a: A double fault occurs when the CPU encounters an exception while trying to handle a previous exception. It typically means the kernel stack has overflowed.

faq.q: Can dust cause this error?
faq.a: Indirectly, yes. Dust buildup can cause overheating, which leads to CPU errors that trigger this BSOD. Clean your PC regularly with compressed air.

faq.q: Does this always mean hardware is failing?
faq.a: Not always. Driver bugs that cause kernel stack overflow are a common cause. However, if software fixes do not help, hardware failure becomes more likely.

title: Boot Device Not Accessible
shortDescription: Windows could not access the system boot device during startup.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when Windows cannot read from the boot device (hard drive or SSD) during the startup process. Common causes include incorrect BIOS settings (IDE vs AHCI), corrupted storage drivers, failed Windows updates that modify boot configuration, or physical drive connection issues.

causes.cause: BIOS changed from AHCI to IDE mode (or vice versa)

symptoms: Blue screen with INACCESSIBLE_BOOT_DEVICE during startup
Windows will not boot at all
Error appears after BIOS update or settings change
BSOD after Windows feature update

fixSteps.title: Check BIOS SATA mode
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS/UEFI and check the SATA mode setting. If it was recently changed, revert it. Windows 10/11 typically requires AHCI mode.

fixSteps.title: Uninstall recent Windows update
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media > Repair your computer > Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > Uninstall Updates.

fixSteps.title: Rebuild BCD
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows media > Command Prompt. Run: bootrec /fixmbr, bootrec /fixboot, bootrec /scanos, bootrec /rebuildbcd

fixSteps.title: Check physical connections
fixSteps.instruction: Power off, open the case, and reseat SATA data and power cables. For NVMe drives, reseat the M.2 SSD. Try a different SATA port if available.

whenToCallPro: If the drive is not detected in BIOS at all, it may have failed and require data recovery services.

faq.q: Can I access my files if I get this error?
faq.a: Usually yes. You can boot from a Windows installation USB and access files through Command Prompt, or connect the drive to another PC as a secondary drive.

faq.q: Why did a Windows update cause this?
faq.a: Some Windows updates replace storage controller drivers. If the new driver is incompatible with your storage controller, Windows can no longer access the boot drive.

faq.q: Does this mean my hard drive is dead?
faq.a: Not usually. The most common cause is a BIOS configuration issue or corrupted boot data, both of which are fixable without replacing hardware.

title: Display Driver Timeout Detection and Recovery Failed
shortDescription: The display driver failed to respond within the timeout period, and recovery was not possible.
fullDescription: TDR (Timeout Detection and Recovery) is a Windows feature that detects when the GPU stops responding and attempts to reset the display driver. This BSOD occurs when TDR fails to recover the GPU. It indicates the GPU driver or hardware is not functioning correctly, often due to driver bugs, GPU overheating, or hardware failure.

causes.cause: GPU overheating due to inadequate cooling

symptoms: Blue screen with VIDEO_TDR_TIMEOUT_DETECTED
Screen goes black momentarily before BSOD
Display flickers or produces artifacts before crash
Crash occurs during gaming or GPU-intensive tasks

fixSteps.title: Clean install GPU drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Download DDU (Display Driver Uninstaller). Boot into Safe Mode, run DDU to completely remove GPU drivers. Reboot and install the latest stable driver from the manufacturer.

fixSteps.title: Monitor GPU temperature
fixSteps.instruction: Install MSI Afterburner or GPU-Z and monitor temperatures. If the GPU exceeds 85C, improve case airflow or clean GPU fans.

fixSteps.title: Remove GPU overclock
fixSteps.instruction: Reset GPU clock speeds and memory speeds to default using MSI Afterburner or the manufacturer's software.

fixSteps.title: Check power supply
fixSteps.instruction: Verify your PSU wattage meets GPU requirements. Ensure all GPU power connectors are firmly seated. Try a different PCIe power cable if available.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after clean driver install, stock clocks, and adequate cooling, the GPU hardware may be failing and needs professional diagnosis.

faq.q: What is TDR?
faq.a: Timeout Detection and Recovery is a Windows feature that detects when the GPU stops responding and attempts to reset the driver. If recovery fails, a BSOD occurs.

faq.q: Should I use the latest GPU driver or an older version?
faq.a: Start with the latest stable driver. If issues persist, try the previous version. Avoid beta drivers unless specifically testing.

faq.q: Can a weak power supply cause this?
faq.a: Yes. If the PSU cannot deliver enough power to the GPU under load, the GPU can become unstable and trigger TDR failures.

title: Display Driver TDR Failure
shortDescription: The display driver failed to recover from a timeout, usually naming the specific driver file (e.g., nvlddmkm.sys, atikmpag.sys).
fullDescription: Similar to VIDEO_TDR_TIMEOUT_DETECTED but this variant typically names the specific display driver file that failed. For NVIDIA cards, the file is usually nvlddmkm.sys; for AMD, it is atikmpag.sys or atikmdag.sys. The troubleshooting approach is the same as VIDEO_TDR_TIMEOUT_DETECTED.

causes.cause: Corrupted or buggy GPU driver (nvlddmkm.sys, atikmpag.sys)

symptoms: Blue screen mentioning nvlddmkm.sys, atikmpag.sys, or igdkmd64.sys
Display freezes then BSOD appears
Screen artifacts appear before crash
Crash during 3D gaming or video playback

fixSteps.title: Clean install GPU driver with DDU
fixSteps.instruction: Boot into Safe Mode, run DDU to fully remove GPU drivers, reboot, install latest stable driver from manufacturer website.

fixSteps.title: Increase TDR timeout (temporary)
fixSteps.instruction: Open Registry Editor. Navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\GraphicsDrivers. Create a DWORD value TdrDelay and set it to 8. This gives the GPU more time to respond.

fixSteps.title: Check GPU thermal paste
fixSteps.instruction: If the GPU is old (3+ years), consider repasting. Remove the GPU cooler, clean old paste, and apply fresh thermal compound.

fixSteps.title: Test with reduced GPU clocks
fixSteps.instruction: Use MSI Afterburner to underclock the GPU core by 100MHz and memory by 200MHz. If crashes stop, the GPU may be degrading.

whenToCallPro: If underclocking stabilizes the GPU, the hardware is degrading. Consider warranty replacement or professional repair.

faq.q: What is nvlddmkm.sys?
faq.a: It is NVIDIA's Windows Display Driver Model kernel-mode driver. It is the core component of NVIDIA GPU drivers on Windows.

faq.q: What is atikmpag.sys?
faq.a: It is AMD's kernel-mode display driver for Radeon GPUs. It is the AMD equivalent of NVIDIA's nvlddmkm.sys.

faq.q: Should I increase TDR timeout permanently?
faq.a: No. Increasing TdrDelay is a temporary workaround to prevent BSODs while troubleshooting. It does not fix the root cause. Address the underlying GPU or driver issue.

title: Corrupted Pool Header in Kernel Memory
shortDescription: The pool header in kernel memory has been corrupted, usually by a driver writing beyond its allocated memory.
fullDescription: This BSOD indicates that a pool header in the Windows kernel memory pool has become corrupted. This occurs when a driver or kernel component writes past the end of its allocated memory block, overwriting the next block's header. Common causes include buggy drivers, faulty RAM, and incompatible software.

causes.cause: Buggy device driver corrupting memory pool
Faulty RAM causing bit flips in pool headers

symptoms: Blue screen with BAD_POOL_HEADER
Random crashes during normal system use
Crash after installing new software or driver
System instability increasing over time

fixSteps.title: Disable third-party antivirus
fixSteps.instruction: Temporarily uninstall third-party antivirus software. Use Windows Defender as a replacement while testing.

fixSteps.title: Update all drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Update all device drivers through Device Manager, focusing on network, storage, and USB drivers.

fixSteps.title: Run memory diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic or MemTest86 to test for RAM errors.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow then DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

whenToCallPro: If the issue persists after driver updates and memory testing, professional minidump analysis can identify the specific pool corruption source.

faq.q: What is the pool in BAD_POOL_HEADER?
faq.a: The pool is a region of kernel memory managed by Windows for dynamic allocations by drivers and the kernel. Each allocation has a header used for tracking.

faq.q: Can this be caused by recently installed software?
faq.a: Yes. Software that installs kernel-mode drivers (antivirus, VPN, disk encryption) can cause pool corruption. Try uninstalling recent additions.

faq.q: Is this a sign of a virus?
faq.a: Unlikely, but some rootkit-type malware can corrupt kernel memory. Run a full scan to be safe, but driver issues are far more common.

title: Invalid Pool Memory Request
shortDescription: A kernel thread made an invalid pool memory request, such as freeing memory that was already freed.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a kernel-mode driver or component makes an invalid request to the memory pool, such as attempting to free memory that has already been freed (double-free), or requesting an allocation with invalid parameters. This is almost always a driver bug.
symptoms: Blue screen with BAD_POOL_CALLER
System crashes randomly or during specific operations
BSOD after driver installation or update
Crash dump references specific driver file

fixSteps.title: Analyze crash dump
fixSteps.instruction: Use BlueScreenView to identify the driver causing the crash from minidump files.

fixSteps.title: Update or roll back the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Update the identified driver to the latest version. If the latest version caused the issue, roll back through Device Manager.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC scan
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow

fixSteps.title: Test RAM
fixSteps.instruction: Run MemTest86 for at least 4 passes to rule out memory hardware issues.

whenToCallPro: If the crash dump does not clearly identify the faulting driver, professional debugging of the crash dump may be required.

faq.q: What is a double-free error?
faq.a: A double-free occurs when code attempts to release (free) a block of memory that has already been released. This corrupts the memory manager's internal tracking structures.

faq.q: Can Windows Update cause this?
faq.a: Yes, if a Windows Update delivers a buggy driver update. Check if the crash started after an update and try rolling it back.

faq.q: Is BAD_POOL_CALLER the same as BAD_POOL_HEADER?
faq.a: They are related but different. BAD_POOL_CALLER means an invalid pool operation was requested. BAD_POOL_HEADER means the pool metadata itself is corrupted.

title: Driver Overran Stack Buffer
shortDescription: A driver wrote past the end of its stack-based buffer, a potential security vulnerability.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a driver writes beyond the bounds of its stack buffer. This is a buffer overflow condition that Windows intentionally catches and halts to prevent potential security exploitation. It is always a driver bug.

causes.cause: Buggy device driver with buffer overflow
Outdated driver incompatible with current Windows

symptoms: Blue screen with DRIVER_OVERRAN_STACK_BUFFER
Crash occurs during specific hardware operations
System becomes unstable after driver update
Random crashes during network or disk activity

fixSteps.title: Identify the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Check the BSOD parameters and minidump files with BlueScreenView to identify the driver.

fixSteps.title: Update or replace the driver
fixSteps.instruction: Download the latest version from the hardware manufacturer. If unavailable, uninstall the device and let Windows use a generic driver.

fixSteps.title: Run malware scan
fixSteps.instruction: Run a full system scan with Windows Defender and Malwarebytes to check for malware exploiting the driver.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Repair system files: sfc /scannow then DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

whenToCallPro: If the driver cannot be identified or if malware is suspected as the cause.

faq.q: Is this a security risk?
faq.a: The BSOD itself is Windows preventing potential exploitation. The underlying driver bug could theoretically be exploited, so updating the driver promptly is important.

faq.q: Can this be caused by malware?
faq.a: While rare, malware that exploits driver vulnerabilities can trigger this. Run security scans alongside driver updates.

faq.q: Does this mean my data is at risk?
faq.a: The BSOD is a protective mechanism that prevents the buffer overflow from being exploited. Your data is safe as long as you update the affected driver.

title: NTFS File System Error
shortDescription: A critical error occurred in the NTFS file system driver (ntfs.sys).
fullDescription: This BSOD indicates a problem with the NTFS file system driver. It can be caused by disk corruption, bad sectors, corrupted NTFS metadata, faulty disk controllers, or damaged cables. It specifically points to ntfs.sys as the failing component.

causes.cause: Faulty SATA cable or disk controller

symptoms: Blue screen referencing ntfs.sys
Disk errors appearing in Event Viewer
Files becoming corrupted or inaccessible
Slow disk performance before crash

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: chkdsk C: /f /r /x. Allow it to complete fully, which may take over an hour.

fixSteps.title: Check drive health
fixSteps.instruction: Use CrystalDiskInfo to check S.M.A.R.T. status. Look for reallocated sectors, pending sectors, and uncorrectable errors.

fixSteps.title: Replace SATA cables
fixSteps.instruction: Power off, open case, and replace the SATA data cable to the affected drive. Try a different SATA port on the motherboard.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow to repair corrupted system files.

whenToCallPro: If S.M.A.R.T. indicates drive failure or if CHKDSK finds uncorrectable errors, data recovery and drive replacement are needed.

faq.q: Does this mean my hard drive is dying?
faq.a: Not necessarily. It can be caused by software corruption, which CHKDSK can often fix. However, check S.M.A.R.T. status to rule out hardware failure.

faq.q: Can I recover data from a drive with this error?
faq.a: Usually yes. Boot from a USB drive or connect the disk to another computer. If the drive has severe physical damage, professional data recovery may be needed.

faq.q: Should I format the drive?
faq.a: Only as a last resort after CHKDSK fails. Formatting erases all data. Try CHKDSK first, and back up important data before formatting.

title: FAT File System Error
shortDescription: A critical error occurred in the FAT file system driver (fastfat.sys).
fullDescription: This BSOD indicates a problem with the FAT file system driver. It commonly occurs when accessing USB drives, SD cards, or older FAT-formatted partitions with corruption. The error is in fastfat.sys, the FAT12/16/32 filesystem driver.

causes.cause: Corrupted FAT-formatted USB drive or SD card
Unsafe removal of external storage device

symptoms: Blue screen when inserting USB drive or SD card
BSOD when accessing external storage
Crash referencing fastfat.sys
External drive shows as RAW instead of FAT32

fixSteps.title: Remove external devices
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect all USB drives, SD cards, and external storage. Reboot to verify the BSOD stops.

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK on the FAT volume
fixSteps.instruction: Reconnect the device. Open elevated Command Prompt: chkdsk E: /f (replace E: with the drive letter).

fixSteps.title: Update USB controller drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager > Universal Serial Bus controllers. Update all USB controller drivers.

fixSteps.title: Format the corrupted device
fixSteps.instruction: If CHKDSK cannot repair the device, back up accessible data, right-click the drive in File Explorer > Format > FAT32 or exFAT.

whenToCallPro: Only if the external device contains critical data that cannot be accessed and needs professional data recovery.

faq.q: Why does this happen with USB drives?
faq.a: USB drives are commonly formatted with FAT32 and are prone to corruption from unsafe removal (unplugging without ejecting). Always use the Safely Remove Hardware option.

faq.q: Can this crash my computer permanently?
faq.a: No. The crash is triggered by the corrupted device. Removing it and rebooting resolves the immediate issue.

faq.q: Should I switch from FAT32 to NTFS?
faq.a: For drives used primarily with Windows, yes. NTFS is more robust against corruption. FAT32 is mainly needed for cross-platform compatibility.

title: Kernel Security Check Failure
shortDescription: The kernel detected the corruption of a critical data structure, indicating a potential security violation.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when the kernel detects that a critical data structure has been corrupted. This is a security mechanism (similar to stack canary checks) that prevents potential exploitation of buffer overflows. It is typically caused by driver bugs, incompatible software, or (rarely) actual security threats.
symptoms: Blue screen with KERNEL_SECURITY_CHECK_FAILURE
Crash during system boot or shortly after login
BSOD after Windows update or driver installation
System instability with frequent crashes

fixSteps.title: Boot into Safe Mode and update drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Boot into Safe Mode. Open Device Manager and update all drivers, especially chipset and storage controller drivers.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow then DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

fixSteps.title: Check for Windows updates
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Settings > Update & Security > Windows Update and install all pending updates.

fixSteps.title: Run memory and disk diagnostics
fixSteps.instruction: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic and chkdsk C: /f /r to check for hardware issues.

whenToCallPro: If the BSOD persists after software repairs and hardware diagnostics pass, professional crash dump analysis is recommended.

faq.q: Is this a security threat?
faq.a: The BSOD is a protective measure that prevents potential exploitation. It is almost always caused by driver bugs rather than actual attacks.

faq.q: Can this be caused by Windows Update?
faq.a: Yes. A Windows Update that installs incompatible drivers can trigger this. Try uninstalling the latest update if the error started after updating.

faq.q: Is my computer hacked?
faq.a: Extremely unlikely. This error is overwhelmingly caused by software bugs, not security breaches. However, running a malware scan is always good practice.

title: APC Index Mismatch
shortDescription: A mismatch in the APC state index was detected, indicating a driver disabled or re-enabled APCs improperly.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when there is a mismatch in the Asynchronous Procedure Call (APC) state index. A driver entered a critical or guarded region and exited improperly, leaving the APC disable count inconsistent. This is a driver bug, commonly associated with audio, display, or filesystem filter drivers.
symptoms: Blue screen with APC_INDEX_MISMATCH
Crash when playing audio or using microphone
BSOD triggered by Skype, Teams, or Zoom
System crashes when plugging in headphones

fixSteps.title: Update audio drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Download the latest audio driver from your motherboard or laptop manufacturer's website. Avoid generic Realtek drivers; use the OEM version.

fixSteps.title: Disable audio enhancements
fixSteps.instruction: Right-click the speaker icon > Sound settings > Device properties > Additional device properties > Enhancements > Disable all enhancements.

fixSteps.title: Update display drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Download and install the latest GPU driver from NVIDIA, AMD, or Intel.

fixSteps.title: Remove conflicting filter drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Temporarily uninstall third-party antivirus and backup software that install filesystem filter drivers.

whenToCallPro: If the crash persists after updating audio and display drivers, professional analysis of the crash dump may be needed.

faq.q: Why does this happen with Realtek audio?
faq.a: Older Realtek audio drivers have known APC handling bugs. Updating to the latest driver from your computer manufacturer usually fixes it.

faq.q: Can video calls trigger this?
faq.a: Yes. Applications that access both audio and video devices simultaneously can expose driver bugs that lead to APC mismatches.

faq.q: What is an APC?
faq.a: An Asynchronous Procedure Call is a function that executes asynchronously in the context of a particular thread. Drivers must properly manage APC disable counts when entering critical regions.

title: Invalid Process Attach Attempt
shortDescription: A thread attempted to attach to a process in an invalid way.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a kernel-mode thread attempts to attach to a process under conditions that are not allowed. This typically happens when a driver calls KeStackAttachProcess while already attached to a different process, or during an APC. It is always a driver bug.

causes.cause: Buggy driver making invalid process attachment calls
Virtualization or sandbox software driver conflict

symptoms: Blue screen with INVALID_PROCESS_ATTACH_ATTEMPT
Crash when running virtual machines or sandboxed applications
BSOD during security software operations
System instability after installing new security software

fixSteps.title: Remove recently installed software
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall any recently installed security, virtualization, or sandboxing software from Settings > Apps.

fixSteps.title: Update all drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager and update all device drivers, focusing on recently changed entries.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow then DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

fixSteps.title: Analyze crash dump
fixSteps.instruction: Use BlueScreenView to identify the specific driver causing the crash.

whenToCallPro: If the faulting driver cannot be identified or belongs to essential software that cannot be removed.

faq.q: Can virtual machine software cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Hypervisor drivers (VMware, VirtualBox, Hyper-V) can conflict and cause this error. Ensure only one hypervisor is active.

faq.q: Is this related to antivirus software?
faq.a: Possibly. Security software with kernel-mode components can trigger this if their drivers have bugs. Try temporarily uninstalling the software.

faq.q: Can I safely ignore this error?
faq.a: No. This BSOD indicates a real driver bug that can lead to data corruption or system instability. Address the underlying cause.

title: Attempted Execution of Non-Executable Memory
shortDescription: A driver attempted to execute code from a memory region marked as non-executable (NX/DEP).
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a driver tries to execute code from a memory page that has been marked as non-executable by Data Execution Prevention (DEP/NX). This is a security protection mechanism. The error typically indicates a driver bug or, rarely, a security exploit attempt.

causes.cause: Buggy driver attempting to execute data as code
Outdated driver incompatible with DEP enforcement

symptoms: Blue screen with ATTEMPTED_EXECUTE_OF_NOEXECUTE_MEMORY
Crash occurs with specific hardware or driver
BSOD referencing a specific .sys driver file
System becomes unstable after driver update

fixSteps.title: Identify and update the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Use BlueScreenView to find the driver responsible. Download the latest version from the manufacturer.

fixSteps.title: Run system file repair
fixSteps.instruction: Run sfc /scannow and DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth from elevated Command Prompt.

fixSteps.title: Run malware scan
fixSteps.instruction: Run a full system scan with Windows Defender and a secondary scanner like Malwarebytes.

fixSteps.title: Check DEP settings
fixSteps.instruction: Open System Properties > Advanced > Performance Settings > Data Execution Prevention. Ensure DEP is set to essential Windows programs and services only if a specific program is causing issues.

whenToCallPro: If the error is suspected to be a security exploit rather than a driver bug, professional security analysis is recommended.

faq.q: What is DEP/NX?
faq.a: Data Execution Prevention (DEP) uses the CPU's No-Execute (NX) feature to mark memory pages as non-executable. This prevents code injection attacks by blocking execution in data regions.

faq.q: Should I disable DEP to fix this?
faq.a: No. Disabling DEP removes an important security protection. Instead, update the faulting driver to one that is DEP-compatible.

faq.q: Is this a virus?
faq.a: Usually not. Most cases are driver bugs. However, if no driver is identified, running thorough security scans is a good precaution.

title: Object Reference Count Mismatch
shortDescription: The reference count of an object is incorrect for the current state of the object.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when the reference count for a Windows kernel object does not match its expected value. This usually means a driver or component decremented a reference count too many times or failed to increment it properly. It is a driver or kernel component bug.

causes.cause: Driver bug with improper object reference handling

symptoms: Blue screen with REFERENCE_BY_POINTER
Random system crashes
Crash occurs when shutting down or restarting
BSOD during file operations or network activity

fixSteps.title: Update all device drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager and update all drivers, focusing on storage, network, and USB controllers.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow then DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

fixSteps.title: Remove recently installed software
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall recently installed applications, especially those with kernel components (VPNs, antivirus, disk encryption).

fixSteps.title: Run System Restore
fixSteps.instruction: Use System Restore to revert to a restore point before the crashes started.

whenToCallPro: If the crash persists after driver updates and system file repairs, professional crash dump analysis may be needed.

faq.q: What is an object reference count?
faq.a: Windows tracks how many components are using an object (like a file or device) using reference counts. When the count reaches zero, the object is freed. A mismatch can cause crashes.

faq.q: Is this a common BSOD?
faq.a: It is less common than many other BSODs but not rare. It typically indicates subtle driver bugs rather than obvious hardware failure.

faq.q: Can this cause data loss?
faq.a: The BSOD itself prevents data loss by halting the system before corruption can spread. However, unsaved work will be lost when the system crashes.

title: Page Frame Number List Corrupted
shortDescription: The Page Frame Number (PFN) database has been corrupted, indicating a serious memory management error.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when the PFN database, which tracks the state of every physical memory page, becomes corrupted. This is a critical error that indicates something overwrote kernel memory management structures. Common causes include buggy drivers, faulty RAM, and Windows system file corruption.

causes.cause: Buggy device driver corrupting PFN database

symptoms: Blue screen with PFN_LIST_CORRUPT
Random crashes during memory-intensive operations
System becomes sluggish before crash
Multiple different BSODs occurring alongside PFN_LIST_CORRUPT

fixSteps.title: Run memory diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic (mdsched.exe) and MemTest86 to test for RAM errors.

fixSteps.title: Update drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Update all device drivers, especially network, storage, and GPU drivers from manufacturer websites.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow then DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

fixSteps.title: Disable third-party antivirus
fixSteps.instruction: Temporarily remove third-party antivirus software and test with Windows Defender only.

whenToCallPro: If RAM tests reveal errors, replacement is needed. If all tests pass but crashes continue, professional minidump analysis is required.

faq.q: What is the PFN database?
faq.a: The Page Frame Number database is a critical Windows data structure that tracks the state of every physical memory page in the system. Its corruption prevents proper memory management.

faq.q: Is this always a hardware problem?
faq.a: No. While faulty RAM is a common cause, driver bugs that overwrite kernel memory structures are equally common. Test both software and hardware.

faq.q: Can this error be intermittent?
faq.a: Yes. The corruption may only occur under specific conditions, making the error appear random. Consistent testing under load can help reproduce it.

title: Driver Power State Failure
shortDescription: A driver failed to respond properly to a power state transition request.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a driver does not properly handle a power state transition (such as sleep, hibernate, wake, or shutdown). The driver fails to complete a power IRP (I/O Request Packet) in a timely manner. This is most common with network adapters, USB devices, and display adapters.

causes.cause: Network adapter driver not handling sleep/wake properly
USB device driver not supporting selective suspend
Power plan settings conflicting with driver capabilities

symptoms: Blue screen when putting the computer to sleep or waking it up
BSOD during shutdown or restart
Screen goes black then BSOD after closing and opening laptop lid
Crash after the system has been idle for a while

fixSteps.title: Update network and display drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Download latest drivers from your hardware manufacturer. For Wi-Fi, check Intel, Qualcomm, or your laptop maker's site.

fixSteps.title: Disable driver power management
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager > Network adapters > right-click adapter > Properties > Power Management > uncheck Allow the computer to turn off this device.

fixSteps.title: Change power plan
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Control Panel > Power Options > Change plan settings > Change advanced power settings. Set PCI Express Link State Power Management to Off.

fixSteps.title: Disable USB selective suspend
fixSteps.instruction: In Power Options advanced settings, expand USB settings > USB selective suspend setting > set to Disabled.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after all power management adjustments, professional driver debugging may be needed.

faq.q: Why does this only happen during sleep/wake?
faq.a: Power state transitions require drivers to suspend and resume their hardware. Drivers with bugs in their power management code fail during these transitions.

faq.q: Can I prevent this by disabling sleep mode?
faq.a: As a workaround, yes. But it is better to update drivers and fix the root cause, as disabling sleep increases power consumption significantly.

faq.q: Does this affect laptops more than desktops?
faq.a: Yes. Laptops use sleep and hibernate more frequently, making power state transition issues more apparent.

title: Clock Watchdog Timeout on Secondary Processor
shortDescription: A secondary processor in a multiprocessor system did not respond to a clock interrupt within the expected time.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when the system detects that a processor core has not responded to a clock interrupt in the allotted time. This indicates one CPU core is hung or unresponsive, commonly due to overclocking instability, CPU overheating, driver deadlocks, or hardware failure.
symptoms: Blue screen with CLOCK_WATCHDOG_TIMEOUT
System completely freezes before BSOD
Crash under heavy CPU load
BSOD appears after overclocking changes

fixSteps.title: Remove overclocking
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS and load optimized defaults. Reset all CPU multipliers, voltages, and base clock to stock values.

fixSteps.title: Check CPU temperatures
fixSteps.instruction: Install HWMonitor and check temperatures under load. Clean CPU cooler and reapply thermal paste if needed.

fixSteps.title: Update BIOS and chipset drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Download latest BIOS update and chipset drivers from your motherboard manufacturer.

fixSteps.title: Run CPU stress test
fixSteps.instruction: Run Prime95 or Intel Burn Test for 30 minutes to verify stability at stock settings. If it fails, hardware may be defective.

whenToCallPro: If the CPU fails stress tests at stock settings with adequate cooling, the CPU or motherboard is likely failing and needs professional evaluation.

faq.q: Does this mean my CPU is broken?
faq.a: Not necessarily. Overclocking is the most common cause. Try stock settings first. If it still occurs with stock settings and good cooling, then the CPU may have issues.

faq.q: Can outdated BIOS cause this?
faq.a: Yes. BIOS updates often include microcode updates that fix CPU stability issues. Always check for BIOS updates, especially after a new CPU installation.

faq.q: Why does this only affect multiprocessor systems?
faq.a: The clock watchdog monitors inter-processor communication. It detects when one core stops responding to the others. Single-core systems would show different symptoms.

title: HAL Initialization Failed
shortDescription: The Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) failed to initialize during system boot.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs very early in the boot process when the Hardware Abstraction Layer cannot initialize properly. The HAL is a critical component that manages communication between Windows and the hardware. This error often indicates corrupted system files, a mismatched HAL version, or boot configuration problems.

causes.cause: Mismatched HAL.dll version after failed update

symptoms: Blue screen during boot with HAL_INITIALIZATION_FAILED
Windows cannot start at all
Error appears after failed Windows upgrade
BSOD occurs very early, before login screen

fixSteps.title: Rebuild BCD
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media > Repair your computer > Command Prompt. Run: bootrec /fixmbr, bootrec /fixboot, bootrec /rebuildbcd

fixSteps.title: Run Startup Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media > Repair your computer > Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > Startup Repair.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC offline
fixSteps.instruction: From Recovery Command Prompt: sfc /scannow /offbootdir=C:\ /offwindir=C:\Windows

fixSteps.title: System Restore
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows media > Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > System Restore. Pick a restore point before the issue.

whenToCallPro: If boot repairs and System Restore fail, a clean Windows installation may be needed. Professional help ensures data is preserved.

faq.q: What is the HAL?
faq.a: The Hardware Abstraction Layer is a Windows component that provides a consistent interface between the OS and hardware, allowing Windows to run on different hardware configurations.

faq.q: Can I fix this without a Windows USB?
faq.a: Unlikely. Since this error prevents booting, you need Windows installation media to access recovery tools.

faq.q: Will I lose my files?
faq.a: The repair steps do not erase personal files. Even if a clean install is needed, you can usually access files by booting from USB first.

title: Boot Volume Cannot Be Mounted
shortDescription: Windows was unable to mount the boot volume to continue startup.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when the Windows I/O subsystem cannot mount the boot volume. This means the system partition exists and can be found, but the file system on it cannot be read. Common causes include corrupted file system, bad sectors on the system drive, or a drive that was not shut down cleanly.

causes.cause: Corrupted file system on boot partition
Bad sectors on the system drive

symptoms: Blue screen with UNMOUNTABLE_BOOT_VOLUME during startup
System cannot boot at all
Error appears after unexpected power loss
Computer was working fine before abrupt shutdown

fixSteps.title: Run Startup Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media > Repair your computer > Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > Startup Repair.

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK from recovery
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows media > Command Prompt. Run: chkdsk C: /f /r. Wait for full completion.

fixSteps.title: Rebuild BCD
fixSteps.instruction: From recovery Command Prompt: bootrec /fixmbr && bootrec /fixboot && bootrec /rebuildbcd

fixSteps.title: Check drive connections
fixSteps.instruction: Power off, open case, reseat SATA and power cables. Try a different SATA port.

whenToCallPro: If CHKDSK reports uncorrectable errors or the drive is not detected, the drive may be failing and data recovery may be needed.

faq.q: Can a power outage cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Abrupt power loss can corrupt the NTFS journal and file system structures, making the boot volume unmountable. CHKDSK usually repairs this.

faq.q: Is my data lost?
faq.a: Usually not. The file system is corrupted but the data is often still intact. CHKDSK can repair the file system in most cases.

faq.q: Do I need a Windows installation USB?
faq.a: Yes. Since Windows cannot boot, you need installation media to access the recovery command prompt and run CHKDSK.

title: Hardware Machine Check Exception
shortDescription: The CPU detected and reported a serious hardware error through the Machine Check Architecture.
fullDescription: This BSOD indicates that the CPU has detected a hardware error through its built-in Machine Check Architecture (MCA). These are the most serious hardware errors - they indicate actual hardware faults in the CPU, memory controller, bus, or cache. Overheating and overclocking are the most common triggers, but genuine hardware failure is also possible.

causes.cause: Motherboard bus or memory controller error

symptoms: Blue screen with MACHINE_CHECK_EXCEPTION
System crashes under heavy CPU load
Event Viewer shows Machine Check errors
Crash accompanied by CPU thermal warnings

fixSteps.title: Reset BIOS to defaults
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS and load optimized defaults. Remove all overclocking.

fixSteps.title: Check and fix cooling
fixSteps.instruction: Clean dust from CPU heatsink and case fans. Verify CPU fan is spinning. Reapply thermal paste if temperatures are high.

fixSteps.title: Update BIOS
fixSteps.instruction: Install the latest BIOS from your motherboard manufacturer. This may include CPU microcode fixes.

fixSteps.title: Run CPU stress test
fixSteps.instruction: Run Prime95 Small FFTs for 30 minutes at stock settings while monitoring temperatures. Any errors indicate hardware problems.

whenToCallPro: If the CPU fails stress tests at stock settings with good cooling, the CPU is likely failing and needs replacement under warranty or professionally.

faq.q: Is this the most serious BSOD?
faq.a: It is among the most serious because it indicates the CPU itself detected a hardware fault. Unlike most BSODs that are software-related, this one almost always points to real hardware issues.

faq.q: Can software cause a Machine Check Exception?
faq.a: Software cannot directly cause a machine check. However, software-controlled overclocking can push hardware beyond its limits, indirectly triggering one.

faq.q: Should I replace my CPU?
faq.a: First try stock settings and proper cooling. If machine checks persist at stock with good thermals, the CPU may need replacement. Check warranty status.

title: Critical Object Terminated Unexpectedly
shortDescription: A process or thread crucial to system operation terminated unexpectedly.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a process or thread that is critical to the operation of Windows terminates unexpectedly. It is similar to CRITICAL_PROCESS_DIED but also covers critical threads. Common causes include failing hard drives, corrupted system files, and driver-induced process crashes.
symptoms: Blue screen with CRITICAL_OBJECT_TERMINATION
System hangs or becomes very slow before BSOD
Clicking sounds from hard drive
Event Viewer shows disk or process errors

fixSteps.title: Check drive health
fixSteps.instruction: Run CrystalDiskInfo to check S.M.A.R.T. status of all drives. Look for reallocated sectors and pending sectors.

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK and SFC
fixSteps.instruction: From elevated Command Prompt: chkdsk C: /f /r then sfc /scannow

fixSteps.title: Check cable connections
fixSteps.instruction: Reseat all SATA data and power cables. Replace cables if they appear worn.

fixSteps.title: Run malware scan
fixSteps.instruction: Run a full system scan with Windows Defender and Malwarebytes.

whenToCallPro: If the drive is failing (bad S.M.A.R.T. status), professional data recovery and drive replacement are recommended.

faq.q: Is this different from CRITICAL_PROCESS_DIED?
faq.a: They are very similar. CRITICAL_OBJECT_TERMINATION covers both critical processes and threads, while CRITICAL_PROCESS_DIED is specific to processes.

faq.q: Can a bad SATA cable cause this?
faq.a: Yes. A loose or damaged SATA cable can cause intermittent disk access failures, which may terminate critical system processes that cannot tolerate I/O errors.

faq.q: Why does my hard drive make clicking sounds?
faq.a: Clicking sounds from a hard drive indicate the read/write head is failing to find data tracks. This is a serious sign of imminent drive failure. Back up data immediately.

title: Kernel Stack Page Could Not Be Read
shortDescription: The requested page of kernel stack data could not be read from the paging file.
fullDescription: Similar to KERNEL_DATA_INPAGE_ERROR, this BSOD occurs when kernel stack data cannot be read from the paging file. The difference is that this specifically affects the kernel stack, which is more critical. The same hardware causes apply: bad sectors, failing drives, faulty RAM, and cable issues.
symptoms: Blue screen with KERNEL_STACK_INPAGE_ERROR
System becomes very slow before crash
Drive activity indicator stays on constantly
Files on disk become corrupted

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: chkdsk C: /f /r

fixSteps.title: Check drive S.M.A.R.T. status
fixSteps.instruction: Use CrystalDiskInfo to check drive health.

fixSteps.title: Test RAM
fixSteps.instruction: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic or MemTest86.

fixSteps.title: Reset paging file
fixSteps.instruction: System Properties > Advanced > Performance > Advanced > Virtual Memory > Change. Set to No paging file, restart, then re-enable.

whenToCallPro: If the drive shows bad S.M.A.R.T. health or CHKDSK finds many bad sectors, drive replacement and data recovery are needed.

faq.q: What is the difference between this and KERNEL_DATA_INPAGE_ERROR?
faq.a: They are very similar. KERNEL_STACK_INPAGE_ERROR specifically affects kernel stack data, while KERNEL_DATA_INPAGE_ERROR affects general kernel data. The troubleshooting is identical.

faq.q: Can this be caused by a corrupted paging file?
faq.a: Yes. Deleting and recreating the paging file can sometimes resolve this if the paging file itself was stored on bad disk sectors.

faq.q: Should I replace my hard drive?
faq.a: Check S.M.A.R.T. status first. If it shows anything other than Good, replace the drive proactively to avoid data loss.

title: Win32k Power Watchdog Timeout
shortDescription: The Win32k display subsystem timed out during a power state transition.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when the Win32k (Windows display) subsystem fails to complete a power state transition within the expected timeframe. This is related to display drivers not properly handling sleep, hibernate, or screen power transitions.

causes.cause: Conflicting display settings or multiple monitors
Windows update breaking display driver compatibility

symptoms: Blue screen when display turns off or on
BSOD when waking from sleep
Crash when connecting or disconnecting external monitor
Screen stays black then BSOD after idle period

fixSteps.title: Update display drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Use DDU to cleanly remove current GPU drivers. Install the latest stable version from the manufacturer.

fixSteps.title: Disable display sleep
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Power Options and set Turn off display to Never as a temporary workaround.

fixSteps.title: Disable hybrid sleep
fixSteps.instruction: Power Options > Advanced > Sleep > Allow hybrid sleep > Off.

fixSteps.title: Check for Windows updates
fixSteps.instruction: Install all pending Windows updates that may include display driver fixes.

whenToCallPro: If clean driver installation does not resolve the issue, professional GPU or display subsystem diagnosis may be needed.

faq.q: Can I just disable display sleep?
faq.a: As a temporary workaround, yes. But the root cause should be fixed with a driver update for a permanent solution.

faq.q: Does this happen more with multiple monitors?
faq.a: Yes. Multiple monitor setups increase the complexity of display power transitions, making driver bugs more likely to manifest.

faq.q: Is this related to VIDEO_TDR_FAILURE?
faq.a: They are related - both involve the display subsystem. However, this error is specifically about power transitions, while TDR is about the GPU not responding during normal operation.

title: Hypervisor Error
shortDescription: The Windows Hyper-V hypervisor encountered a fatal error.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when the Hyper-V hypervisor encounters a condition it cannot recover from. It can be caused by conflicting virtualization software, incompatible CPU features, corrupted Hyper-V files, or BIOS virtualization settings.
symptoms: Blue screen with HYPERVISOR_ERROR
Crash when starting virtual machines
BSOD after enabling Hyper-V or WSL2
System crashes when running Docker or Windows Sandbox

fixSteps.title: Check BIOS virtualization settings
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS. Enable Intel VT-x or AMD-V, and enable VT-d/IOMMU if available.

fixSteps.title: Remove conflicting hypervisors
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall VMware Workstation or VirtualBox if Hyper-V is needed. Only one hypervisor can be active at a time.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall Hyper-V
fixSteps.instruction: Open Windows Features (optionalfeatures.exe), uncheck Hyper-V, restart, then re-enable it and restart again.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow to repair system files.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after enabling BIOS virtualization and reinstalling Hyper-V, professional Windows configuration may be needed.

faq.q: Can I use VMware and Hyper-V at the same time?
faq.a: Recent versions of VMware Workstation can coexist with Hyper-V, but older versions cannot. VirtualBox also has limited Hyper-V compatibility. Check software documentation.

faq.q: Do I need Hyper-V enabled?
faq.a: Hyper-V is needed for WSL2, Docker Desktop, Windows Sandbox, and Hyper-V virtual machines. If you do not use these, you can disable it.

faq.q: Can low RAM cause this?
faq.a: The hypervisor requires memory overhead. Systems with 4GB or less may struggle with virtualization. 8GB or more is recommended.

title: Storage Miniport Driver Error
shortDescription: A storage miniport driver returned an unexpected status during volume open.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a storage miniport driver returns a reparse status when opening a volume, which is not allowed. This indicates a bug in the storage driver, commonly affecting RAID controllers, iSCSI initiators, or third-party storage filter drivers.
symptoms: Blue screen with DRIVER_RETURNED_STATUS_REPARSE_FOR_VOLUME_OPEN
BSOD during boot when accessing storage volumes
Crash after RAID configuration change
Error after installing storage management software

fixSteps.title: Update storage controller drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Download the latest storage controller driver from the RAID card or motherboard manufacturer.

fixSteps.title: Remove third-party storage software
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall any third-party disk management, storage filter, or RAID management software.

fixSteps.title: Roll back storage driver
fixSteps.instruction: In Device Manager > Storage controllers, right-click the controller > Properties > Driver > Roll Back Driver.

fixSteps.title: Update RAID firmware
fixSteps.instruction: If using a hardware RAID controller, check for firmware updates from the controller manufacturer.

whenToCallPro: If you are using enterprise storage (RAID arrays, iSCSI, SAN) and driver updates do not help, professional storage administration support is recommended.

faq.q: Is this common on home PCs?
faq.a: No. This error is more common on systems with RAID controllers, iSCSI storage, or enterprise storage configurations. Home PCs rarely encounter it.

faq.q: Can this cause data loss?
faq.a: The BSOD itself does not cause data loss, but if the underlying storage driver issue persists, it could lead to data corruption. Address it promptly.

faq.q: Should I rebuild my RAID array?
faq.a: No. This error is a driver issue, not a RAID degradation issue. Updating the driver should fix it without touching the array.

title: Windows Driver Framework Violation
shortDescription: A driver using the Windows Driver Framework committed a fatal framework violation.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a WDF (Windows Driver Framework) driver commits a violation that the framework catches. WDF is a modern driver development framework, so this error typically points to relatively recent drivers. USB and Bluetooth drivers are common causes.

causes.cause: Windows update breaking WDF driver compatibility

symptoms: Blue screen with WDF_VIOLATION
Crash when connecting USB devices
BSOD when enabling Bluetooth
System crashes when using specific peripherals

fixSteps.title: Disconnect USB devices and test
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect all non-essential USB devices and Bluetooth peripherals. Test if the BSOD stops.

fixSteps.title: Update USB and Bluetooth drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager and update all Universal Serial Bus controllers and Bluetooth drivers.

fixSteps.title: Run Windows Update
fixSteps.instruction: Install all pending updates which may include WDF framework updates.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall USB controllers
fixSteps.instruction: In Device Manager, uninstall all USB Root Hub and USB Host Controller entries. Restart to let Windows reinstall them.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after driver updates and the faulting driver is identified as a system driver that cannot be easily replaced.

faq.q: What is WDF?
faq.a: Windows Driver Framework is a set of libraries and tools for developing Windows device drivers. It provides a higher-level abstraction than the older WDM (Windows Driver Model).

faq.q: Can a USB hub cause this?
