faq.a: Yes. Powered USB hubs with buggy firmware or incompatible drivers are a known trigger. Try connecting devices directly to the computer.

faq.q: Is this specific to Windows 10/11?
faq.a: WDF is present in Windows 8.1 and later, so this BSOD can occur on those versions. It does not affect Windows 7 systems using older WDM drivers.

title: Resource Not Owned
shortDescription: A thread attempted to release a resource it did not own.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a kernel thread tries to release a resource (like a lock or mutex) that it does not currently own. This is a programming error in a driver or kernel component that indicates improper synchronization handling.
symptoms: Blue screen with RESOURCE_NOT_OWNED
Random crashes during file operations
System hangs before BSOD
Crash occurs during multi-threaded workloads

fixSteps.title: Analyze crash dump
fixSteps.instruction: Use BlueScreenView to identify the faulting driver from minidump files.

fixSteps.title: Update all drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Update all device drivers, focusing on storage and filesystem filter drivers.

fixSteps.title: Run memory diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: Test RAM with Windows Memory Diagnostic or MemTest86.

fixSteps.title: Remove third-party filter drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall antivirus, backup, and encryption software that install kernel filter drivers.

whenToCallPro: If crash dump analysis does not reveal the faulting driver, professional kernel debugging is needed.

faq.q: What is a kernel resource?
faq.a: A kernel resource is a synchronization mechanism (like an executive resource or mutex) used to control access to shared data structures in the kernel.

faq.q: Is this a common BSOD?
faq.a: No. It is relatively uncommon and usually indicates a specific driver bug rather than a general system problem.

faq.q: Can this be caused by antivirus software?
faq.a: Yes. Antivirus and backup software that use filesystem filter drivers are known to have synchronization bugs that can trigger this error.

title: Multiple IRP Complete Requests
shortDescription: A driver attempted to complete an I/O Request Packet (IRP) that was already completed.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a driver calls IoCompleteRequest on an IRP (I/O Request Packet) that has already been completed. This is always a driver bug - the driver is completing the same I/O operation twice, which corrupts the I/O manager's internal structures.

causes.cause: Driver bug completing I/O requests twice
Race condition in driver I/O handling

symptoms: Blue screen with MULTIPLE_IRP_COMPLETE_REQUESTS
Crash during heavy disk I/O
BSOD triggered by specific file operations
System instability with storage-intensive tasks

fixSteps.title: Identify the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Use BlueScreenView to analyze crash dumps and identify the driver responsible.

fixSteps.title: Update the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Download and install the latest version of the identified driver from the manufacturer.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow

fixSteps.title: Run Driver Verifier
fixSteps.instruction: From elevated Command Prompt: verifier /standard /all. This will catch the double-completion bug. Run verifier /reset when done.

whenToCallPro: If Driver Verifier catches the bug but no updated driver is available, the driver vendor needs to fix the bug.

faq.q: What is an IRP?
faq.a: An I/O Request Packet is a kernel data structure used to communicate I/O requests between the I/O manager and device drivers. Each IRP should be completed exactly once.

faq.q: Can this cause data corruption?
faq.a: Yes. Completing an IRP twice can corrupt the I/O manager's structures and potentially lead to data corruption on disk. The BSOD prevents this.

faq.q: Is Driver Verifier safe to use?
faq.a: Driver Verifier stress-tests drivers and may cause additional BSODs if drivers have bugs. Use it for diagnosis only and disable it (verifier /reset) after identifying the faulting driver.

title: IRQL Not at Dispatch Level
shortDescription: A kernel operation that requires DISPATCH_LEVEL IRQL was performed at an incorrect IRQL.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a kernel operation that must run at DISPATCH_LEVEL IRQL is executed at a different IRQL. This is always a driver or kernel component programming error. It is relatively rare compared to IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL.

causes.cause: Driver bug with incorrect IRQL management

symptoms: Blue screen with IRQL_NOT_DISPATCH_LEVEL
Random system crashes
Crash during specific hardware operations
BSOD after driver update

fixSteps.title: Identify and update the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Check minidump files with BlueScreenView and update the identified driver.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow then DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth

fixSteps.title: Remove recently installed software
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall software installed shortly before the crashes began.

fixSteps.title: System Restore
fixSteps.instruction: Use System Restore to revert to a state before the crashes started.

whenToCallPro: If the faulting driver cannot be identified through minidump analysis.

faq.q: What is DISPATCH_LEVEL?
faq.a: DISPATCH_LEVEL is an interrupt request level in Windows where thread scheduling occurs. Certain kernel operations can only be performed at this level.

faq.q: How is this different from IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL?
faq.a: IRQL_NOT_LESS_OR_EQUAL occurs when IRQL is too high to access paged memory. IRQL_NOT_DISPATCH_LEVEL is about an operation requiring a specific IRQL that was not met.

faq.q: Is this a rare error?
faq.a: Yes, it is less common than most IRQL-related BSODs. It typically affects specific driver versions with IRQL management bugs.

title: Hardware Data Bus Error
shortDescription: A parity error on the system data bus was detected, indicating a hardware memory fault.
fullDescription: This BSOD indicates a hardware parity error was detected on the system memory bus. This is almost always caused by faulty RAM, though it can also be caused by motherboard bus failures. The error means data was corrupted during transfer between the CPU and RAM.
symptoms: Blue screen with DATA_BUS_ERROR
System crashes randomly, especially under memory load
Error occurs after adding new RAM
Memory-related errors in Event Viewer

fixSteps.title: Run memory diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: Run MemTest86 from a bootable USB for at least 8 passes to thoroughly test RAM.

fixSteps.title: Reseat RAM modules
fixSteps.instruction: Power off, open case, remove all RAM sticks, clean contacts with a dry cloth, and reseat firmly.

fixSteps.title: Test individual RAM sticks
fixSteps.instruction: Install one RAM stick at a time and test. Identify which module causes the error.

fixSteps.title: Check RAM compatibility
fixSteps.instruction: Verify your RAM is on the motherboard's QVL (Qualified Vendor List). Check speeds and timings match the motherboard specifications.

whenToCallPro: If all RAM sticks individually test fine but the error persists, the motherboard memory controller or bus may be failing.

faq.q: What is a parity error?
faq.a: A parity error means data was corrupted during transfer on the memory bus. The system detected that the data read from RAM does not match what was written.

faq.q: Can I fix faulty RAM?
faq.a: No. Faulty RAM modules must be replaced. Check if your RAM is still under warranty for a free replacement.

faq.q: Does mixing RAM brands cause this?
faq.a: It can. Mixing RAM with different speeds, timings, or voltages can cause bus errors. For best stability, use identical modules.

title: No More IRP Stack Locations
shortDescription: An I/O request was sent to a driver with no remaining IRP stack locations available.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a higher-level driver sends an I/O request down the driver stack but there are no more stack locations available for the next lower driver. This typically happens when there are too many filter drivers layered on top of each other.

causes.cause: Too many filesystem filter drivers (antivirus, backup, encryption)
Driver miscalculating required IRP stack size

symptoms: Blue screen with NO_MORE_IRP_STACK_LOCATIONS
Crash when multiple security products are active
BSOD during file operations with encrypted volumes
System becomes unstable after installing additional security software

fixSteps.title: Remove extra security software
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall redundant antivirus, anti-malware, and endpoint protection products. Keep only one antivirus solution.

fixSteps.title: Remove unnecessary filter drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall backup agents, disk encryption, and storage management software that install filter drivers.

fixSteps.title: Increase IRP stack size in registry
fixSteps.instruction: Open Registry Editor. Navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters. Create or modify IRPStackSize DWORD and set to 18-50.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: sfc /scannow

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after removing filter drivers, professional driver stack analysis is needed.

faq.q: What is an IRP stack?
faq.a: Each I/O request in Windows has a stack of entries, one for each driver it passes through. When a system has many layered filter drivers, this stack can run out of space.

faq.q: Can running two antivirus programs cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Multiple antivirus products each add filesystem filter drivers. This is a well-known cause of IRP stack exhaustion.

faq.q: What is a safe IRPStackSize value?
faq.a: The default is 15. Values between 18 and 50 are generally safe. Do not set it above 50 as excessive values waste kernel memory.

title: Thread Stuck in Device Driver
shortDescription: A thread in a device driver is stuck in an infinite loop, commonly the display driver.
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when a device driver thread enters an infinite loop, most commonly the display driver. The system detects that the thread has been spinning for too long without yielding. This is closely related to VIDEO_TDR_FAILURE but can affect non-display drivers as well.

causes.cause: Display driver infinite loop or hang
Driver bug triggered by specific workload

symptoms: Blue screen with THREAD_STUCK_IN_DEVICE_DRIVER
Screen freezes completely before BSOD
Display artifacts appear before crash
System hangs during graphics-intensive operations

fixSteps.title: Clean install GPU drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Boot into Safe Mode, run DDU to remove all GPU drivers, reboot, and install the latest stable version.

fixSteps.title: Check GPU temperatures
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor GPU temperature with MSI Afterburner. Clean GPU fans and heatsink if temperatures are high.

fixSteps.title: Remove GPU overclock
fixSteps.instruction: Reset GPU core and memory clocks to default values.

fixSteps.title: Test GPU stability
fixSteps.instruction: Run FurMark or 3DMark to stress test the GPU and watch for crashes or artifacts.

whenToCallPro: If the GPU fails stress tests at stock clocks with good cooling, the GPU hardware may be failing.

faq.q: Is this always a GPU problem?
faq.a: Usually, but not always. While the display driver is the most common culprit, any device driver can get stuck in a loop.

faq.q: Can this damage my GPU?
faq.a: The BSOD itself does not damage hardware. However, if overheating is the cause, continued operation at high temperatures can degrade the GPU over time.

faq.q: Should I try an older GPU driver?
faq.a: If the latest driver causes issues, trying the previous stable version is a valid troubleshooting step. Some driver versions have known bugs with specific GPU models.

title: Special Pool Detected Memory Corruption
shortDescription: Driver Verifier detected that a driver corrupted memory in the special pool.
fullDescription: This BSOD is generated by Driver Verifier's special pool feature when it detects that a driver has written beyond the bounds of its allocated memory. This error only occurs when Driver Verifier is enabled and means a specific driver has a memory corruption bug.

causes.cause: Driver writing beyond allocated buffer (detected by Verifier)
Outdated driver with known buffer overflow
Driver incompatible with current Windows version

symptoms: Blue screen with SPECIAL_POOL_DETECTED_MEMORY_CORRUPTION while Driver Verifier is running
Crash identifies specific driver file
Error occurs only when Driver Verifier is enabled
System is stable without Driver Verifier

fixSteps.title: Note the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: The BSOD message identifies exactly which driver corrupted memory. Record this information.

fixSteps.title: Update or remove the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Download the latest version of the identified driver. If no update is available, consider alternative hardware or drivers.

fixSteps.title: Disable Driver Verifier
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: verifier /reset. Restart the computer.

fixSteps.title: Report the bug to driver vendor
fixSteps.instruction: Contact the hardware manufacturer and report the Driver Verifier finding with the crash dump.

whenToCallPro: If the faulting driver is essential and no updated version is available, professional driver debugging or alternative solutions may be needed.

faq.q: Should I keep Driver Verifier running?
faq.a: No. Driver Verifier is a diagnostic tool that adds overhead and can cause additional BSODs. Disable it after identifying the problematic driver.

faq.q: Will this crash happen without Driver Verifier?
faq.a: The driver bug exists regardless, but without Verifier it may not crash immediately. Instead, it silently corrupts memory, which can lead to other BSODs later.

faq.q: Can I ignore this if my system works without Verifier?
faq.a: The driver still has a bug that corrupts memory. While it may not crash often, it can cause random instability and data corruption. Updating the driver is recommended.

title: System Out of Memory
shortDescription: The system ran out of memory (both physical RAM and virtual memory/page file).
fullDescription: This BSOD occurs when Windows completely exhausts all available memory, including physical RAM and the page file. The system can no longer allocate memory for critical operations and halts. This can be caused by memory leaks in applications or drivers, insufficient RAM for the workload, or a page file that is too small or disabled.

causes.cause: Page file disabled or too small

symptoms: Blue screen with STATUS_NO_MEMORY
System becomes extremely slow before crash
Applications fail to launch or crash with out-of-memory errors
Task Manager shows high memory usage
Disk activity at 100% from paging

fixSteps.title: Enable and size page file properly
fixSteps.instruction: Go to System Properties > Advanced > Performance Settings > Advanced > Virtual Memory. Set page file to System managed or 1.5x your RAM size.

fixSteps.title: Identify memory-leaking applications
fixSteps.instruction: Open Task Manager > Details tab > right-click column headers > add Commit Size. Sort by Commit Size to find memory hogs.

fixSteps.title: Update drivers to fix leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Update all drivers, especially GPU, network, and storage drivers which are common sources of kernel memory leaks.

fixSteps.title: Add more RAM
fixSteps.instruction: If your system consistently uses over 80% of RAM during normal use, consider adding more physical memory.

whenToCallPro: If memory leaks are identified but the source driver or application cannot be determined, professional performance analysis may be needed.

faq.q: How much RAM do I need?
faq.a: For general use, 8GB is minimum. For gaming and multitasking, 16GB is recommended. For professional workloads, 32GB or more may be needed.

faq.q: Should I disable the page file?
faq.a: No. Disabling the page file can cause this BSOD. Even with ample RAM, Windows uses the page file for stability. Keep it enabled.

faq.q: What is a memory leak?
faq.a: A memory leak occurs when software allocates memory but fails to release it when done. Over time, this consumes all available memory until the system runs out.

title: Unexpected Store Exception in Kernel
shortDescription: An unexpected exception occurred in the store component, typically related to storage hardware or driver issues.
fullDescription:
The UNEXPECTED_STORE_EXCEPTION blue screen indicates that the store component encountered an unexpected exception. This error is frequently associated with failing hard drives or SSDs, outdated storage drivers, or file system corruption.

This BSOD became notably more common after certain Windows 10 feature updates and is often linked to antivirus software conflicts or issues with fast startup. Users with older SSDs or mechanical hard drives approaching end of life are particularly susceptible to this error.

In many cases, the error can be resolved by updating storage drivers, disabling fast startup, or running disk health checks. If the storage device is failing, replacement may be necessary.

causes.cause: File system corruption on boot volume

symptoms: Blue screen with UNEXPECTED_STORE_EXCEPTION
System crashes during disk-intensive operations
Frequent crashes after Windows update
Slow boot times before eventual crash
SMART warnings in disk health tools

fixSteps.title: Check disk health
fixSteps.instruction: Open Command Prompt as administrator and run: wmic diskdrive get status. Also install CrystalDiskInfo to check SMART data for warnings or reallocated sectors.

fixSteps.title: Update storage drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager, expand Disk drives and Storage controllers. Right-click each device and select Update driver. Also check the manufacturer website for firmware updates.

fixSteps.title: Disable Fast Startup
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Control Panel > Power Options > Choose what the power buttons do > Change settings that are currently unavailable. Uncheck Turn on fast startup.

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt and run: chkdsk C: /f /r. Schedule the check for next restart and reboot. This will scan for and repair file system errors and bad sectors.

fixSteps.title: Temporarily disable antivirus
fixSteps.instruction: If using third-party antivirus, temporarily disable it or uninstall it to test if the BSOD stops. Windows Defender will provide basic protection in the meantime.

whenToCallPro: If SMART data shows the drive is failing or CHKDSK finds many bad sectors, a professional can help with data recovery before replacing the drive.

faq.q: Is UNEXPECTED_STORE_EXCEPTION always a hard drive problem?
faq.a: No. While failing storage is common, this error can also be caused by antivirus conflicts, driver issues, or Windows update bugs.

faq.q: Can an SSD cause this error?
faq.a: Yes. SSDs can develop issues, especially older models with outdated firmware. Check SMART data and update firmware from the manufacturer.

faq.q: Should I replace my hard drive?
faq.a: Only if diagnostic tools show the drive is failing. Check SMART status, run CrystalDiskInfo, and look for reallocated sectors or pending sectors.

title: Video Scheduler Internal Error
shortDescription: The video scheduler has detected a fatal violation, indicating a critical GPU driver or hardware failure.
fullDescription:
This BSOD occurs when the video scheduler detects a fatal violation within the GPU subsystem. The video scheduler is responsible for managing GPU command buffers and ensuring the graphics card responds to commands in a timely manner.

The most common cause is a corrupted, outdated, or incompatible GPU driver. Overclocked GPUs, overheating graphics cards, and hardware defects can also trigger this error. It frequently appears during gaming, video playback, or other graphics-intensive tasks.

Resolving this error typically involves performing a clean GPU driver installation using DDU (Display Driver Uninstaller), then installing the latest stable driver from the GPU manufacturer.
symptoms: Blue screen with VIDEO_SCHEDULER_INTERNAL_ERROR
Crashes during gaming or video playback
Screen artifacts or flickering before crash
Display driver stopped responding messages before BSOD
System freezes with buzzing audio

fixSteps.title: Clean install GPU drivers with DDU
fixSteps.instruction: Download DDU (Display Driver Uninstaller) and the latest GPU driver. Boot into Safe Mode, run DDU to completely remove current drivers, then install the fresh driver.

fixSteps.title: Remove GPU overclock
fixSteps.instruction: If using MSI Afterburner, EVGA Precision, or similar, reset all clocks, voltages, and fan curves to default. Restart and test stability.

fixSteps.title: Check GPU temperatures
fixSteps.instruction: Install HWMonitor or GPU-Z and monitor GPU temperature under load. If temperature exceeds 90C, clean dust from the GPU cooler and case fans, or reapply thermal paste.

fixSteps.title: Test with integrated graphics
fixSteps.instruction: If your CPU has integrated graphics, remove the discrete GPU or disable it in BIOS and test. If crashes stop, the GPU hardware may be faulty.

fixSteps.title: Increase TDR timeout
fixSteps.instruction: Open Registry Editor and navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\GraphicsDrivers. Create a DWORD value named TdrDelay and set it to 8 (seconds).

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after a clean driver install and the GPU passes stress tests, a professional can diagnose whether the GPU hardware itself is failing or if there is a motherboard issue.

faq.q: Can this error damage my GPU?
faq.a: The BSOD itself does not damage the GPU. However, the underlying cause (like overheating) could cause long-term damage if not addressed.

faq.q: Should I use the latest or previous GPU driver?
faq.a: Try the latest stable (non-beta) driver first. If issues persist, try the previous version. Some driver releases are known to cause issues with specific games or hardware.

faq.q: What is DDU and is it safe?
faq.a: DDU (Display Driver Uninstaller) is a trusted utility that completely removes GPU drivers and residual files. It is safe and widely recommended for clean driver installations.

title: Faulty Hardware Corrupted Page
shortDescription: A hardware component has corrupted a page of memory, indicating RAM or CPU issues.
fullDescription:
The FAULTY_HARDWARE_CORRUPTED_PAGE BSOD indicates that a hardware component — most commonly RAM — has corrupted data in a memory page. Windows detected that a page of memory expected to contain specific data was altered unexpectedly, pointing to a hardware fault.

This error can also be caused by motherboard issues, CPU cache errors, or in some cases, driver bugs that corrupt memory. It became more prevalent with certain Intel and AMD platform chipset issues.

Diagnosis requires thorough hardware testing, starting with RAM and extending to the CPU and motherboard if RAM tests pass.

causes.cause: CPU cache error or overheating CPU
Motherboard chipset or memory controller issue
RAM overclocking or XMP profile instability

symptoms: Blue screen with FAULTY_HARDWARE_CORRUPTED_PAGE
Random crashes with no specific trigger pattern
Data corruption in files
Applications crash with memory-related errors
System instability increases over time

fixSteps.title: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic
fixSteps.instruction: Press Win+R, type mdsched.exe, restart and check for problems. After reboot, check Event Viewer > Windows Logs > System for MemoryDiagnostics results.

fixSteps.title: Run MemTest86 extended test
fixSteps.instruction: Download MemTest86, create a bootable USB, and run the full test suite for at least 4 passes. This takes several hours but is more thorough than Windows Memory Diagnostic.

fixSteps.title: Disable XMP/DOCP profile
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS/UEFI and disable XMP (Intel) or DOCP (AMD) memory profiles. Run RAM at JEDEC default speeds and test for stability.

fixSteps.title: Test individual RAM sticks
fixSteps.instruction: Remove all but one RAM stick and test. Rotate through each stick individually to identify the faulty module. Also test each DIMM slot.

fixSteps.title: Update BIOS and chipset drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Check your motherboard manufacturer website for BIOS updates and chipset drivers. Some BIOS updates fix memory controller stability issues.

whenToCallPro: If MemTest86 passes but the error persists, a professional can perform advanced diagnostics on the CPU, motherboard, and memory controller to identify the fault.

faq.q: Does this always mean my RAM is bad?
faq.a: Not always, but RAM is the most common cause. Run MemTest86 first. If RAM passes, check CPU temperatures and BIOS/chipset updates.

faq.q: Can software cause this error?
faq.a: Rarely. While a driver bug could theoretically corrupt memory, this error almost always points to hardware. Focus on hardware diagnostics first.

faq.q: Should I replace all my RAM or just the bad stick?
faq.a: If testing identifies one faulty stick, replace it. For best compatibility, consider replacing the entire kit with a matched set from your motherboard QVL list.

title: Attempted Write to Read-Only Memory
shortDescription: A driver attempted to write to a memory segment marked as read-only.
fullDescription:
This BSOD occurs when a driver or kernel-mode component attempts to write to a memory region that has been marked as read-only. This is a memory protection violation that indicates a buggy driver or corrupted system component.

The error most commonly occurs after installing new drivers, particularly graphics, network, or storage drivers. It can also appear after Windows updates that introduce driver compatibility issues.

The minidump file typically identifies the specific driver responsible. Clean installation of the offending driver usually resolves the issue.

causes.cause: Hardware fault causing memory protection failures

symptoms: Blue screen with ATTEMPTED_WRITE_TO_READONLY_MEMORY
Crash occurs shortly after driver installation
Minidump references a specific .sys driver file
System may crash during boot if the driver loads at startup
Crash during specific hardware operations

fixSteps.title: Identify the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Check the BSOD screen for a file name (e.g., nvlddmkm.sys). Also open Event Viewer > Windows Logs > System and look for BugCheck events that name the responsible driver.

fixSteps.title: Roll back or update the driver
fixSteps.instruction: In Device Manager, find the device associated with the faulting driver. Right-click > Properties > Driver tab > Roll Back Driver. If no rollback is available, update to the latest version from the manufacturer.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt. Run: DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth, then: sfc /scannow. This repairs any corrupted system files.

fixSteps.title: Boot into Safe Mode to remove driver
fixSteps.instruction: If the system cannot boot, use Windows Recovery Environment to boot into Safe Mode. Then uninstall the problematic driver from Device Manager.

whenToCallPro: If you cannot identify the faulting driver or the error occurs with multiple drivers, a professional can analyze minidump files to determine the root cause.

faq.q: Which driver is causing this error?
faq.a: The BSOD screen or minidump file usually names the .sys file responsible. Common culprits include GPU drivers (nvlddmkm.sys, atikmpag.sys), network drivers, and antivirus kernel drivers.

faq.q: Can I fix this without Safe Mode?
faq.a: If Windows boots normally, yes. Update or roll back the driver through Device Manager. If Windows cannot boot, you will need Safe Mode or the Recovery Environment.

faq.q: Is this a sign of RAM failure?
faq.a: Usually not. This error is about software-level memory protection, not physical memory failure. However, if it occurs randomly without a clear driver cause, running a memory test is worthwhile.

title: Pool Corruption in File Area
shortDescription: A driver corrupted pool memory in the area used for file system operations.
fullDescription:
This BSOD indicates that a kernel-mode component has corrupted the memory pool in the area allocated for file system operations. Pool memory is used by drivers and the kernel for temporary allocations, and corruption in the file area specifically suggests a storage-related driver issue.

The error typically points to a buggy file system filter driver, such as those used by antivirus programs, backup software, or disk encryption tools. Storage controller drivers and RAID drivers can also be responsible.

Resolving this error usually requires identifying and updating or removing the offending filter driver.

causes.cause: Buggy file system filter driver (antivirus, backup, encryption)

symptoms: Blue screen with POOL_CORRUPTION_IN_FILE_AREA
Crashes during file operations (copying, saving, deleting)
System instability after installing backup or security software
Corruption in recently saved files
Crash during antivirus scans

fixSteps.title: Identify file system filter drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt and run: fltmc. This lists all file system filter drivers. Note any third-party filters from antivirus, backup, or encryption software.

fixSteps.title: Update or remove suspect filter drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Update antivirus, backup, and encryption software to latest versions. If the problem started after installing specific software, temporarily uninstall it to test.

fixSteps.title: Update storage controller drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Check Device Manager > Storage controllers and IDE ATA/ATAPI controllers. Update all drivers, particularly RAID or AHCI drivers, from the manufacturer website.

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK on all volumes
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt and run chkdsk on each drive: chkdsk C: /f /r. This checks for file system corruption and bad sectors.

whenToCallPro: If you cannot identify the problematic filter driver or the error persists after removing third-party software, a professional can analyze kernel dumps and filter driver stacks.

faq.q: What is a file system filter driver?
faq.a: Filter drivers sit between applications and the file system, intercepting file operations. Antivirus real-time scanning, backup agents, and disk encryption all use filter drivers.

faq.q: Can antivirus software cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Antivirus real-time protection uses file system filter drivers. A buggy update to the antivirus can cause pool corruption. Try updating or temporarily removing the antivirus.

faq.q: Is my data at risk?
faq.a: Pool corruption can lead to file system corruption in severe cases. Back up important data as soon as possible and run CHKDSK to verify file system integrity.

title: Kernel Mode Heap Corruption
shortDescription: The kernel detected heap corruption, typically caused by a buggy driver or firmware issue.
fullDescription:
The KERNEL_MODE_HEAP_CORRUPTION BSOD indicates that the Windows kernel detected corruption in a kernel-mode heap. This is a serious error that means a driver or kernel component has written to memory it should not have, corrupting the heap data structures.

This error is often caused by drivers that perform incorrect memory operations, such as writing beyond allocated buffer boundaries (buffer overflows) or using memory after it has been freed (use-after-free). Firmware bugs, particularly in UEFI firmware, can also be responsible.

The error is typically reproducible and linked to specific hardware or driver combinations. Identifying the responsible driver through dump analysis is key to resolution.

causes.cause: Buggy kernel-mode driver with memory corruption bug

symptoms: Blue screen with KERNEL_MODE_HEAP_CORRUPTION
Crashes may occur during specific operations or randomly
System instability increases over time
Other heap-related errors may also appear
Possible data corruption

fixSteps.title: Update all drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager and update all drivers, particularly GPU, network, storage, and USB drivers. Download latest versions from manufacturer websites rather than using Windows Update.

fixSteps.title: Update BIOS/UEFI firmware
fixSteps.instruction: Visit your motherboard or laptop manufacturer website. Download the latest BIOS/UEFI update and follow their instructions to flash it.

fixSteps.title: Analyze minidump for faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Install WinDbg from the Microsoft Store. Open the latest dump file from C:\Windows\Minidump and run: !analyze -v to identify the responsible driver.

fixSteps.title: Run memory diagnostics
fixSteps.instruction: Run Windows Memory Diagnostic (mdsched.exe) and MemTest86 to rule out hardware memory issues.

whenToCallPro: If dump analysis does not clearly identify the faulting driver, or if BIOS updates and driver updates do not resolve the issue, a professional kernel debugger can perform live analysis.

faq.q: What is heap corruption?
faq.a: Heap corruption occurs when code writes to memory outside the boundaries of its allocation, damaging the heap data structures. This can cause crashes, data corruption, or security vulnerabilities.

faq.q: Can this be caused by malware?
faq.a: While possible, this error is much more commonly caused by legitimate driver bugs. However, run a full malware scan to rule it out.

faq.q: Is this related to RAM failure?
faq.a: Usually not. This is a software-level corruption issue. However, flaky RAM can produce similar symptoms, so run MemTest86 to rule it out.

title: Secure Boot Violation Detected
shortDescription: The Secure Boot policy was violated by an unsigned or improperly signed boot component.
fullDescription:
This BSOD occurs when Windows detects that a component loaded during the boot process violates the Secure Boot policy. Secure Boot ensures that only trusted, digitally signed software can run during the boot process, protecting against rootkits and boot-level malware.

The error can be triggered by unsigned or improperly signed boot loaders, drivers, or firmware components. Dual-boot configurations with Linux, custom boot managers, or modified boot components are common causes.

Resolving this typically involves ensuring all boot components are properly signed, or adjusting Secure Boot settings in UEFI firmware.

causes.cause: Unsigned boot loader from dual-boot setup
BIOS/UEFI firmware issue with Secure Boot keys

symptoms: Blue screen with SECURE_BOOT_VIOLATION
System fails to boot after changing boot configuration
Error appears after adding dual-boot OS
Boot failure after BIOS update
Security software warnings about boot integrity

fixSteps.title: Check Secure Boot status
fixSteps.instruction: Press Win+R, type msinfo32, look for Secure Boot State. Also enter UEFI settings and check Secure Boot configuration.

fixSteps.title: Repair boot components
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media, open Command Prompt from Recovery, and run: bootrec /fixmbr, bootrec /fixboot, bootrec /rebuildbcd.

fixSteps.title: Reset Secure Boot keys
fixSteps.instruction: Enter UEFI firmware settings and find Secure Boot options. Select Reset to Default or Install Default Keys to restore the default Microsoft Secure Boot keys.

fixSteps.title: Run malware scan from recovery
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media or a bootable antivirus USB. Run an offline scan to check for rootkits or boot-level malware.

whenToCallPro: If you suspect boot-level malware or cannot restore proper Secure Boot configuration, a professional can perform thorough boot integrity analysis.

faq.q: Can I disable Secure Boot?
faq.a: Yes, you can disable Secure Boot in UEFI settings. However, Windows 11 requires Secure Boot, and disabling it reduces protection against boot-level malware.

faq.q: Does dual-booting Linux cause this?
faq.a: It can if the Linux boot loader is not signed with a trusted key. Most modern Linux distributions support Secure Boot via the shim bootloader.

faq.q: Is this a sign of malware?
faq.a: It can be, but it is more often caused by misconfigured boot components. Run an offline malware scan to be safe.

title: Non-Maskable Interrupt Hardware Failure
shortDescription: A non-maskable interrupt (NMI) was triggered by a hardware failure.
fullDescription:
The NMI_HARDWARE_FAILURE BSOD is triggered when hardware generates a Non-Maskable Interrupt, which is a high-priority interrupt that cannot be ignored by the processor. This typically indicates a serious hardware problem.

Common causes include failing RAM, motherboard issues, overheating components, or power supply problems. The NMI can also be triggered by parity errors in memory, PCI bus errors, or I/O channel checks.

This error usually requires hardware diagnostics to identify the failing component. The issue is rarely software-related.
symptoms: Blue screen with NMI_HARDWARE_FAILURE
System crashes under heavy load
Random crashes with no software pattern
Beep codes from BIOS during POST
System fails to boot intermittently

fixSteps.title: Check system temperatures
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS/UEFI and check CPU temperature, or install HWMonitor in Windows. High idle temperatures (above 70C for CPU) indicate cooling issues.

fixSteps.title: Run memory diagnostics
fixSteps.instruction: Run MemTest86 from a bootable USB for at least 4 passes. This tests for the parity errors that commonly trigger NMI.

fixSteps.title: Reseat hardware components
fixSteps.instruction: Power off, unplug, and open the case. Reseat RAM, GPU, and all expansion cards. Check all power connectors are firmly seated.

fixSteps.title: Test with minimal configuration
fixSteps.instruction: Remove all non-essential hardware (extra RAM sticks, GPU, expansion cards, USB devices). Boot with only one RAM stick and integrated graphics to isolate the fault.

fixSteps.title: Test power supply
fixSteps.instruction: If available, test with a known-good power supply or use a PSU tester. Unstable voltage from a failing PSU can trigger NMI errors.

whenToCallPro: If basic diagnostics (RAM test, temperature check, reseating) do not identify the issue, a professional can perform board-level diagnostics to find the failing component.

faq.q: What is a Non-Maskable Interrupt?
faq.a: An NMI is a hardware interrupt that the CPU cannot ignore. It is reserved for critical hardware errors like memory parity failures and I/O channel checks.

faq.q: Is this always a hardware problem?
faq.a: Almost always. NMI errors are triggered by hardware, though rarely a BIOS bug or misconfiguration can cause them.

faq.q: Can this be fixed without replacing hardware?
faq.a: Sometimes. Reseating components, cleaning dust, or updating BIOS can fix the issue. But if a component is truly failing, replacement is necessary.

title: Critical Registry Error
shortDescription: A severe registry error occurred, indicating registry hive corruption or I/O failure.
fullDescription:
The REGISTRY_ERROR BSOD indicates that the Windows registry encountered a critical error, typically due to registry hive corruption or a failure reading/writing registry data to disk. The registry is a critical database that stores system and application configuration.

This error can be caused by disk failures that prevent registry hive files from being read, abrupt power loss that corrupts in-memory registry data, or malware that damages registry structures.

Recovery typically involves restoring registry hives from backup or using System Restore to return to a working state.

causes.cause: Registry hive file corruption on disk
Disk I/O error preventing registry access
Abrupt power loss during registry write

symptoms: Blue screen with REGISTRY_ERROR
System fails to boot or boot loops
Error occurs during startup or shutdown
Event log shows registry-related errors
Programs fail to open or settings reset

fixSteps.title: Use System Restore
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows Recovery Environment. Go to Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > System Restore. Select a restore point from before the error began.

fixSteps.title: Restore registry from backup
fixSteps.instruction: Boot to Recovery Environment, open Command Prompt. Copy backup registry hives: copy C:\Windows\System32\config\RegBack\* C:\Windows\System32\config\. If RegBack is empty (common in Windows 10), use System Restore instead.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: From Recovery Environment Command Prompt, run: DISM /Image:C:\ /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth, then: sfc /scannow /offbootdir=C:\ /offwindir=C:\Windows.

fixSteps.title: Check disk health
fixSteps.instruction: Run chkdsk C: /f /r from the Recovery Environment to check for disk errors that may be preventing registry file access.

whenToCallPro: If System Restore fails and registry backup restoration does not work, a professional can attempt manual registry repair or data recovery.

faq.q: Can I fix a corrupted registry manually?
faq.a: Manual registry editing is risky and can make things worse. Use System Restore or registry backup restoration first. Manual editing should only be done as a last resort.

faq.q: Will reinstalling Windows fix this?
faq.a: Yes, a clean install will create a fresh registry. However, try System Restore and registry backup restoration first to avoid data loss.

faq.q: Should I use registry cleaners?
faq.a: No. Registry cleaners can cause more harm than good. Microsoft does not recommend using third-party registry cleaners.

title: Kernel Auto Boost Lock at Raised IRQL
shortDescription: A lock was acquired at a raised IRQL with auto-boost enabled, causing a kernel deadlock condition.
fullDescription:
This BSOD occurs when the kernel detects that a lock acquisition with auto-boost was performed at a raised IRQL, which can lead to deadlock. This is a programming error in a kernel-mode driver.

The error was introduced in newer Windows versions as part of enhanced lock validation. It typically points to a driver that has incorrect synchronization logic.

The faulting driver must be identified through dump analysis and updated or replaced.

causes.cause: Buggy kernel driver with incorrect lock handling
Outdated driver incompatible with current Windows version

symptoms: Blue screen with KERNEL_AUTO_BOOST_LOCK_ACQUISITION_WITH_RAISED_IRQL
System freezes then crashes
Crash occurs during specific hardware operations
May be more frequent under high system load

fixSteps.title: Analyze minidump for faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: Install WinDbg from the Microsoft Store. Open the minidump file from C:\Windows\Minidump and run !analyze -v to identify the driver.

fixSteps.title: Update the identified driver
fixSteps.instruction: Once the faulting driver is identified, download the latest version from the hardware manufacturer website and install it.

fixSteps.title: Update Windows
fixSteps.instruction: Install all pending Windows Updates, including optional driver updates. Microsoft sometimes ships driver fixes through Windows Update.

fixSteps.title: Remove third-party security software
fixSteps.instruction: If the faulting driver belongs to security software, uninstall it using the vendor's dedicated removal tool and test stability.

whenToCallPro: If you cannot identify the faulting driver from the minidump or the latest driver version still causes the error, a professional can perform detailed kernel debugging.

faq.q: What is IRQL?
faq.a: IRQL (Interrupt Request Level) is a priority mechanism in Windows. Higher IRQL code has restrictions on what operations it can perform. Violating these rules causes BSODs.

faq.q: Can I fix this myself?
faq.a: If you can identify the driver from the minidump and update it, yes. Otherwise, dump analysis requires technical knowledge.

faq.q: Is this dangerous for my computer?
faq.a: The BSOD itself prevents damage by stopping the system. However, the underlying driver bug could potentially cause data corruption in rare cases.

title: ACPI BIOS Error
shortDescription: The ACPI BIOS of the computer is not fully compliant with the ACPI specification.
fullDescription:
The ACPI_BIOS_ERROR BSOD indicates that the computer's ACPI (Advanced Configuration and Power Interface) BIOS is not fully compliant with the ACPI specification. ACPI is the standard for power management and hardware configuration.

This error is most common on older hardware, systems with outdated BIOS firmware, or after a BIOS update that introduced ACPI table errors. Laptops are more frequently affected due to their complex power management requirements.

Updating the BIOS firmware is the primary fix. If no BIOS update is available, ACPI-related boot parameters may work around the issue.

causes.cause: Outdated BIOS firmware with ACPI bugs
BIOS update introduced ACPI table errors
Incompatible hardware with current Windows version

symptoms: Blue screen with ACPI_BIOS_ERROR during Windows installation or boot
System fails to sleep or hibernate properly
Power management features not working
Error appears after BIOS update
Laptop fans running at full speed constantly

fixSteps.title: Update BIOS firmware
fixSteps.instruction: Visit your PC or motherboard manufacturer website. Download the latest BIOS update and follow the manufacturer's flashing instructions carefully.

fixSteps.title: Reset BIOS to defaults
fixSteps.instruction: Enter BIOS/UEFI setup, find the option to Load Optimized Defaults or Reset to Default Settings. Save and exit.

fixSteps.title: Disable ACPI in BIOS if possible
fixSteps.instruction: Some BIOS versions allow disabling ACPI or switching to a legacy mode. This is a workaround, not a fix, and may disable power management features.

fixSteps.title: Use boot parameter workaround
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media, open Command Prompt, and try: bcdedit /set {current} acpimode legacy. This forces legacy ACPI mode.

whenToCallPro: If BIOS updates do not resolve the issue and the hardware is needed for a specific purpose, a professional may be able to patch ACPI tables or identify compatibility workarounds.

faq.q: What is ACPI?
faq.a: ACPI (Advanced Configuration and Power Interface) is a standard that allows the operating system to manage power consumption and hardware configuration.

faq.q: Is updating BIOS risky?
faq.a: There is a small risk — if the update is interrupted, the system may not boot. Use a UPS, follow manufacturer instructions exactly, and do not interrupt the process.

faq.q: Can old hardware run Windows 11 with this error?
faq.a: If the BIOS cannot be updated to support Windows 11 ACPI requirements, the hardware may not be compatible. Consider staying on Windows 10 or upgrading hardware.

title: Session Has Valid Views on Exit
shortDescription: A session was terminated while it still had valid views (mapped memory sections).
fullDescription:
The SESSION_HAS_VALID_VIEWS_ON_EXIT BSOD indicates that a session driver did not properly clean up mapped views before the session ended. This is a session management error typically caused by a third-party driver.

This error is most commonly seen with Remote Desktop, virtual machine, or multi-session configurations. Terminal Services and Citrix environments are particularly affected.

Identifying and updating the responsible session driver usually resolves the issue.

causes.cause: Third-party session driver not cleaning up views
Remote Desktop or Citrix driver bug
Printer driver loaded in session space

symptoms: Blue screen with SESSION_HAS_VALID_VIEWS_ON_EXIT
Crash when remote desktop sessions disconnect
BSOD on terminal server or Citrix server
Error during user logoff
Crash when virtual machine display changes

fixSteps.title: Update Remote Desktop and virtualization components
fixSteps.instruction: If running RDP, update Remote Desktop client and server components. For Citrix, update VDA (Virtual Delivery Agent) to the latest version.

fixSteps.title: Update printer drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Update or remove third-party printer drivers, especially those used in redirected printer sessions. Use Windows built-in drivers as a test.

fixSteps.title: Update display and video drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Update all display drivers, particularly virtual display adapters used by VMs or remote sessions.

fixSteps.title: Analyze crash dump
fixSteps.instruction: Use WinDbg to open the minidump and identify the specific driver that did not clean up its views.

whenToCallPro: If this occurs on a production server or Citrix environment, a professional system administrator should analyze the dumps and coordinate with the software vendor for a fix.

faq.q: Is this common on regular PCs?
faq.a: No, this error is rare on standard desktops. It primarily affects servers and multi-user environments running Remote Desktop or Citrix.

faq.q: Can printer drivers cause this?
faq.a: Yes, particularly printer drivers that are loaded in session space for redirected printing in Remote Desktop or Citrix sessions.

faq.q: Does this affect only the user who triggered it?
faq.a: No. Since this is a kernel BSOD, the entire system crashes, affecting all users on the server.

title: Cache Manager Error
shortDescription: The file system cache manager encountered a critical error.
fullDescription:
The CACHE_MANAGER BSOD indicates that the Windows file system cache manager detected an inconsistency or error in cached file data. The cache manager is responsible for caching file reads and writes to improve disk performance.

This error is typically caused by exhaustion of non-paged pool memory, file system driver bugs, or storage hardware issues that corrupt cached data. Antivirus filter drivers that interfere with cache operations can also trigger this.

Resolving this error usually involves updating storage and filter drivers, checking disk health, and ensuring adequate system memory.

causes.cause: File system driver or filter driver bug

symptoms: Blue screen with CACHE_MANAGER
System crashes during heavy file I/O operations
Performance degradation before crash
File copy operations fail or corrupt data
High non-paged pool usage in Task Manager

fixSteps.title: Check non-paged pool usage
fixSteps.instruction: Open Task Manager > Performance > Memory. Check Non-paged pool size. If it is unusually high (over 1GB), a driver is likely leaking non-paged pool memory.

fixSteps.title: Update storage and filter drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Update all storage controller drivers, file system filter drivers (antivirus, backup), and chipset drivers to the latest versions.

fixSteps.title: Run disk health checks
fixSteps.instruction: Run chkdsk C: /f /r on all drives. Also check SMART data with CrystalDiskInfo for early warning signs of disk failure.

fixSteps.title: Identify pool leak source
fixSteps.instruction: If non-paged pool is high, use poolmon.exe (from Windows SDK) or RAMMap to identify which driver tag is consuming the most pool memory.

whenToCallPro: If pool memory leaks are identified but the responsible driver cannot be determined, or if disk health checks reveal hardware issues, professional diagnosis may be needed.

faq.q: What is the cache manager?
faq.a: The cache manager is a Windows component that caches recently accessed file data in RAM for faster access. It improves performance by reducing disk reads.

faq.q: Can low RAM cause this error?
faq.a: Yes, if the system has insufficient RAM, the cache manager may not have enough memory to operate correctly. 8GB is minimum for modern Windows.

faq.q: Is my data at risk?
faq.a: There is some risk of file corruption if cached writes were not flushed to disk. Run CHKDSK and verify important files after resolving the error.

title: No More System Page Table Entries
shortDescription: The system ran out of page table entries, exhausting virtual address space.
fullDescription:
The NO_MORE_SYSTEM_PTES BSOD occurs when Windows exhausts its supply of system page table entries (PTEs). PTEs are used to map virtual memory to physical memory. When they run out, the system can no longer allocate memory for kernel operations.

This error is most common on 32-bit systems where virtual address space is limited. On 64-bit systems, it can still occur if a driver leaks PTEs by mapping memory but never unmapping it.

The primary fix involves identifying and updating the driver that is leaking system PTEs.

causes.cause: Driver leaking system PTEs (not unmapping memory)
32-bit Windows running out of virtual address space
Too many mapped sections consuming PTEs
Antivirus or backup driver leaking mapped memory

symptoms: Blue screen with NO_MORE_SYSTEM_PTES
System slows down before crash
Applications fail to allocate memory
Error becomes more frequent the longer the system runs
Restarting temporarily resolves the issue

fixSteps.title: Update all drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Update all drivers to the latest versions, particularly storage, network, and security drivers which commonly map kernel memory.

fixSteps.title: Upgrade to 64-bit Windows
fixSteps.instruction: If running 32-bit Windows, back up data and perform a clean install of 64-bit Windows 10 or 11 to gain a much larger virtual address space.

fixSteps.title: Increase system PTEs via registry
fixSteps.instruction: Open Registry Editor, navigate to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\Memory Management. Set SystemPages DWORD to 0 (auto-calculate maximum).

fixSteps.title: Remove suspect drivers
fixSteps.instruction: If the error started after installing specific software, uninstall it and its drivers. Antivirus and VPN software are common culprits.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after driver updates and you cannot identify the leaking driver, a professional can use kernel debugging tools to trace PTE allocations.

faq.q: What are page table entries?
faq.a: PTEs are data structures used by the CPU to translate virtual memory addresses to physical memory addresses. Each mapped page of memory requires a PTE.

faq.q: Is this more common on 32-bit systems?
faq.a: Yes. 32-bit Windows has a very limited system PTE space. Upgrading to 64-bit Windows dramatically increases the available PTEs.

faq.q: Why does restarting fix it temporarily?
faq.a: Restarting clears the PTE leak. The leaking driver starts fresh with no leaked PTEs. Over time, the leak grows until PTEs are exhausted again.

title: Mismatched HAL Detected
shortDescription: The Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) does not match the kernel or system configuration.
fullDescription:
The MISMATCHED_HAL BSOD indicates that the Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) does not match the kernel or the machine type. The HAL is the layer between Windows and the hardware, and a mismatch means the wrong HAL is loaded for the system.

This error commonly occurs after cloning a disk from one computer to another with different hardware, modifying boot configuration data incorrectly, or mixing system files from different Windows installations.

Repairing the boot configuration or reinstalling Windows on the target hardware typically resolves this.

causes.cause: Disk cloned to hardware with different HAL requirements
System files replaced from incompatible Windows installation

symptoms: Blue screen with MISMATCHED_HAL during boot
System fails to boot after disk migration
Error after replacing motherboard
Boot failure after editing BCD store
Crash during Windows setup on new hardware

fixSteps.title: Repair boot configuration
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media, open Command Prompt, run: bootrec /rebuildbcd. Also try: bcdboot C:\Windows to recreate the boot files.

fixSteps.title: Run Startup Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media. Select Repair your computer > Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > Startup Repair.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: From Recovery Environment, run: DISM /Image:C:\ /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth and sfc /scannow /offbootdir=C:\ /offwindir=C:\Windows to replace any mismatched system files.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall Windows
fixSteps.instruction: If repairs fail, perform a clean Windows installation on the target hardware. Back up data first using a Linux live USB or Windows Recovery file copy.

whenToCallPro: If you need to migrate a Windows installation to different hardware without reinstalling, a professional can use sysprep and proper migration tools.

faq.q: What is the HAL?
faq.a: The Hardware Abstraction Layer is a Windows component that provides a consistent interface between the OS and different hardware platforms. It handles differences in CPU, interrupt controllers, and timers.

faq.q: Can I move a hard drive to a new computer?
faq.a: Modern Windows 10/11 handles hardware changes well, but older versions may get this error. Use sysprep before moving the drive to generalize the installation.

faq.q: Does this error mean my hardware is broken?
faq.a: No. This is a software mismatch, not a hardware failure. The wrong version of HAL is loaded for the hardware.

title: Configuration List Failed
shortDescription: Windows could not load the registry hive file, often due to disk corruption or missing files.
fullDescription:
The CONFIG_LIST_FAILED BSOD occurs when Windows cannot load one of the critical registry hive files during startup. The registry hives (SYSTEM, SOFTWARE, SAM, SECURITY, DEFAULT) are essential files that must be loaded for Windows to function.

This error is typically caused by disk corruption affecting the registry files, sudden power loss during a registry write operation, or accidental deletion of registry hive files.

Recovery usually requires restoring registry hives from a backup or using System Restore from the Recovery Environment.

causes.cause: Corrupted registry hive file on disk
Sudden power loss during registry write
Disk sector failure in registry file location
Accidental deletion or modification of registry files

symptoms: Blue screen with CONFIG_LIST_FAILED during boot
Windows fails to start completely
Error appears after power outage
System boot loops
Unable to enter Safe Mode

fixSteps.title: Use System Restore from Recovery
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media or Recovery Environment. Go to Troubleshoot > Advanced Options > System Restore and select a restore point.

fixSteps.title: Restore registry hives from backup
fixSteps.instruction: In Recovery Command Prompt, copy backup hives: copy C:\Windows\System32\config\RegBack\SYSTEM C:\Windows\System32\config\SYSTEM. Repeat for SOFTWARE, SAM, SECURITY, DEFAULT.

fixSteps.title: Run CHKDSK on the boot drive
fixSteps.instruction: From Recovery Command Prompt, run: chkdsk C: /f /r. This fixes disk errors that may have corrupted the registry files.

fixSteps.title: Perform in-place repair install
fixSteps.instruction: If registry restoration fails, boot from Windows installation media and select Upgrade to repair the Windows installation while keeping files and applications.

whenToCallPro: If registry backups are not available and System Restore has no valid restore points, a professional can attempt manual registry recovery or in-place repair.

faq.q: Can I prevent this error?
faq.a: Enable System Restore and ensure it has sufficient disk space for restore points. Also use a UPS to prevent sudden power loss during writes.

faq.q: Are my files lost?
faq.a: Your personal files are likely intact. The error affects registry hives, not user data files. You can boot from a Linux live USB to copy files off the drive.

faq.q: What if RegBack is empty?
faq.a: Windows 10 version 1803+ may not automatically back up registry hives to RegBack. Use System Restore instead, or enable the RegIdleBackup task manually.

title: Assign Drive Letters Failed
shortDescription: Windows could not assign drive letters to disk partitions during boot.
fullDescription:
The ASSIGN_DRIVE_LETTERS_FAILED BSOD occurs when Windows is unable to assign drive letters to disk partitions during the boot process. This is a critical failure because Windows needs drive letter assignments to access the boot and system volumes.

The error is commonly caused by disk configuration changes, such as adding or removing drives, changing partition layouts, or issues with the disk subsystem. SAN or RAID configuration changes can also trigger this.

Repairing the boot configuration and disk assignments usually resolves the issue.
symptoms: Blue screen with ASSIGN_DRIVE_LETTERS_FAILED during boot
Error appears after connecting or disconnecting drives
Boot failure after RAID configuration change
System cannot find boot volume
Disk Management shows incorrect partition layout

fixSteps.title: Restore original disk configuration
fixSteps.instruction: If drives were recently added or removed, restore the original configuration. Reconnect missing drives or disconnect newly added ones.

fixSteps.title: Repair boot configuration
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media, open Command Prompt, run: bootrec /fixmbr, bootrec /fixboot, bootrec /rebuildbcd.

fixSteps.title: Use diskpart to verify partitions
fixSteps.instruction: From Recovery Command Prompt, run diskpart, then: list disk, select disk 0, list partition, list volume. Verify partitions and volumes are visible.

fixSteps.title: Manually assign drive letters
fixSteps.instruction: In diskpart: select volume X, assign letter=C. Ensure the Windows volume has the correct drive letter assignment.

whenToCallPro: If the partition table is corrupted or a RAID configuration needs to be rebuilt, a professional can use advanced disk recovery tools.

faq.q: Can adding a new drive cause this?
faq.a: Yes, adding or removing drives can change the disk enumeration order, potentially conflicting with boot drive letter assignments.

faq.q: Is my data safe?
faq.a: Usually yes. The partitions and data are likely intact; only the drive letter assignments are confused. Recovery is usually possible without data loss.

faq.q: Should I use MBR or GPT?
faq.a: For UEFI systems (most modern PCs), use GPT. For legacy BIOS systems, use MBR. Mixing the wrong type can cause boot issues.

title: Kernel Thread Priority Floor Violation
shortDescription: A kernel thread had its priority improperly lowered below the expected floor level.
fullDescription:
This BSOD indicates that a kernel thread's priority was set below the established priority floor. This is a kernel-mode programming error where a driver or system component violates thread scheduling rules.

The error is relatively uncommon and was added in newer Windows versions as part of enhanced kernel validation. It typically points to a specific driver with a threading bug.

Updating or replacing the faulting driver resolves the issue.

causes.cause: Driver with incorrect thread priority handling
Outdated driver incompatible with Windows scheduler changes
Third-party system utility modifying thread priorities

symptoms: Blue screen with KERNEL_THREAD_PRIORITY_FLOOR_VIOLATION
Crashes during specific driver operations
May occur more often under high CPU load
System instability after installing specific software

fixSteps.title: Identify faulting driver from dump
fixSteps.instruction: Open the minidump file with WinDbg and run !analyze -v to identify which driver caused the priority violation.

fixSteps.title: Update all drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Update all system drivers from manufacturer websites, focusing on recently installed or updated drivers.

fixSteps.title: Remove system optimization utilities
fixSteps.instruction: Uninstall any third-party system optimization, game boost, or process priority tools that may interfere with thread scheduling.

fixSteps.title: Install latest Windows updates
fixSteps.instruction: Run Windows Update to install all pending updates including cumulative and driver updates.

whenToCallPro: If you cannot identify the faulting driver or updates do not resolve the issue, a professional can perform kernel-level debugging.

faq.q: What is thread priority in Windows?
faq.a: Thread priority determines the order in which the CPU scheduler allocates time to running threads. Higher priority threads get more CPU time.

faq.q: Can game boosters cause this?
faq.a: Yes. Game boosters and process priority tools may improperly modify kernel thread priorities, triggering this violation.

faq.q: Is this error dangerous?
faq.a: The BSOD prevents further damage by stopping the system. The underlying driver bug could theoretically cause issues, but the check catches it early.

title: Invalid Work Queue Item
shortDescription: An invalid item was found in a system work queue, indicating memory corruption.
fullDescription:
The INVALID_WORK_QUEUE_ITEM BSOD occurs when Windows finds an invalid entry in a kernel work queue. Work queues are used to schedule deferred work in the kernel, and an invalid item means the queue data structure has been corrupted.

This is typically caused by a driver that corrupts memory adjacent to work queue structures. Buffer overflows, use-after-free bugs, or race conditions in drivers can lead to this corruption.

The error requires identifying and fixing the driver responsible for the memory corruption.

causes.cause: Hardware memory issue corrupting kernel data

symptoms: Blue screen with INVALID_WORK_QUEUE_ITEM
Random crashes with no clear pattern
System instability that worsens over time
Other memory corruption BSODs may also appear
Crash may occur during system idle or shutdown

fixSteps.title: Update all drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Update all drivers from manufacturer websites. Focus on network, storage, and USB drivers which commonly use work queues.

fixSteps.title: Enable Driver Verifier
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt, run: verifier /standard /all. Reboot and wait for a crash that identifies the specific faulting driver. Disable verifier after: verifier /reset.

fixSteps.title: Run memory diagnostics
fixSteps.instruction: Run MemTest86 for multiple passes to rule out hardware memory corruption causing the queue corruption.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth then sfc /scannow to repair system files.

whenToCallPro: If Driver Verifier does not catch the faulting driver and memory tests pass, a professional can perform advanced kernel debugging to trace the corruption source.

faq.q: What is a work queue?
faq.a: A work queue is a kernel mechanism for deferring work to a worker thread. Drivers queue work items that are processed later at a lower IRQL.

faq.q: What is Driver Verifier?
faq.a: Driver Verifier is a Windows tool that applies extra checking to drivers to catch bugs like buffer overflows and pool corruption. It may cause additional BSODs that identify the buggy driver.

faq.q: Can this cause data loss?
faq.a: Memory corruption errors can potentially cause data loss if corrupted data is written to disk. Back up important data and resolve the issue quickly.

title: USB Driver Bug Code
shortDescription: A critical error was detected in a USB driver.
fullDescription:
The BUGCODE_USB_DRIVER BSOD indicates that a critical error occurred in a USB driver. This can be a Microsoft USB stack driver or a third-party USB device driver. The error typically involves invalid operations on USB requests or data structures.

Common causes include faulty USB devices, outdated USB host controller drivers, power management issues with USB ports, and damaged USB cables. This error is also common when USB selective suspend causes issues.

Resolving this usually involves updating USB drivers, disabling USB selective suspend, or identifying and removing the problematic USB device.

causes.cause: Faulty USB device causing driver errors
USB selective suspend power management issue

symptoms: Blue screen with BUGCODE_USB_DRIVER
Crash when connecting or disconnecting USB devices
BSOD during sleep/resume with USB devices connected
System crashes when using specific USB device
USB device stops working before crash

fixSteps.title: Disconnect USB devices and test
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect all USB devices except keyboard and mouse. Use the system normally. If crashes stop, reconnect devices one at a time to identify the culprit.

fixSteps.title: Disable USB selective suspend
fixSteps.instruction: Go to Control Panel > Power Options > Change plan settings > Change advanced > USB settings > USB selective suspend setting. Set to Disabled for both On battery and Plugged in.

fixSteps.title: Update USB drivers
fixSteps.instruction: Open Device Manager, expand Universal Serial Bus controllers. Right-click each USB host controller and Root Hub, select Update Driver. Also check manufacturer website for chipset USB drivers.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall USB controllers
fixSteps.instruction: In Device Manager, right-click each USB controller and select Uninstall device. Restart the computer to let Windows reinstall them.

fixSteps.title: Try different USB port or cable
fixSteps.instruction: If a specific device triggers the crash, try a different USB port (USB 2.0 vs 3.0) or replace the cable.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after testing all USB devices and updating drivers, the USB host controller hardware on the motherboard may be faulty.

faq.q: Can a USB drive cause a BSOD?
faq.a: Yes. Faulty USB flash drives, external hard drives, and other USB devices can trigger this BSOD if they cause driver errors.

faq.q: What is USB selective suspend?
faq.a: It is a power management feature that allows Windows to suspend individual USB ports to save power. It can cause issues with some USB devices.

faq.q: Should I use USB 2.0 or 3.0 ports?
faq.a: If a device causes crashes on USB 3.0, try USB 2.0 as a troubleshooting step. Some devices have compatibility issues with USB 3.0 controllers.

title: Driver Unloaded Without Cancelling Pending Operations
shortDescription: A driver was unloaded while it still had pending operations, causing system instability.
fullDescription:
This BSOD occurs when a driver is unloaded from memory while it still has pending I/O operations, timers, DPCs (Deferred Procedure Calls), or other callbacks. When these callbacks execute, they reference code that no longer exists in memory, causing a crash.

This is a driver programming error. The driver's unload routine failed to cancel all pending operations before the driver was removed. It commonly occurs when uninstalling or updating drivers.

The fix involves identifying the driver and updating it, or ensuring proper cleanup during driver removal.

causes.cause: Driver update process not cleaning up old driver

symptoms: Blue screen with DRIVER_UNLOADED_WITHOUT_CANCELLING_PENDING_OPERATIONS
Crash occurs after uninstalling hardware or software
BSOD during driver update process
System crashes when disconnecting hardware
Minidump references a driver .sys file

fixSteps.title: Identify the faulting driver
fixSteps.instruction: The BSOD screen typically shows the driver name. Check Event Viewer > Windows Logs > System for BugCheck events or use WinDbg to analyze the minidump.

fixSteps.title: Clean install the driver
fixSteps.instruction: Completely uninstall the identified driver using Device Manager or the vendor's uninstaller, reboot, then install the latest version.

fixSteps.title: Remove leftover driver files
fixSteps.instruction: After uninstalling, check C:\Windows\System32\drivers for leftover .sys files from the old driver. Also use pnputil /enum-drivers to list third-party drivers.

fixSteps.title: Use safe removal for hardware
fixSteps.instruction: Always use Safely Remove Hardware before disconnecting USB devices. For internal hardware, power off before removing components.

whenToCallPro: If the error persists after clean driver installation, a professional can analyze the exact pending operations and determine if the driver vendor needs to issue a fix.

faq.q: Why does this happen during driver updates?
faq.a: Driver updates involve unloading the old driver and loading the new one. If the old driver does not properly cancel its pending operations during unload, this BSOD occurs.

faq.q: Can I prevent this?
faq.a: Ensure you use proper uninstallation procedures. Use vendor-provided uninstallers and restart when prompted. Avoid forcefully removing hardware during operation.

faq.q: Is the driver permanently broken?
faq.a: No. The error occurs during the unload transition. A clean install of the latest driver version should work properly during normal operation.

title: FTDisk Internal Error
shortDescription: An internal error occurred in the FTDisk (Fault Tolerant Disk) system, typically related to RAID or dynamic disk issues.
fullDescription:
The FTDISK_INTERNAL_ERROR BSOD indicates a failure in the Windows Fault Tolerant Disk subsystem. This subsystem handles dynamic disks, software RAID configurations (mirrored, striped, and RAID-5 volumes), and partition management.

The error typically occurs when a dynamic disk configuration becomes corrupted, a member of a RAID set fails, or the FTDisk driver encounters an inconsistency in the disk metadata.

Resolving this may require repairing the dynamic disk configuration, replacing failed disks, or converting back to basic disks.

causes.cause: Failed member of software RAID set

symptoms: Blue screen with FTDISK_INTERNAL_ERROR
BSOD during boot with dynamic disk configuration
Dynamic disks showing Foreign or Failed status
Software RAID volumes not mounting
Disk Management shows errors for dynamic volumes

fixSteps.title: Boot from Recovery Environment
fixSteps.instruction: Boot from Windows installation media and open Command Prompt from Troubleshoot > Advanced Options.

fixSteps.title: Check disk status with diskpart
fixSteps.instruction: Run diskpart, then: list disk and list volume. Check for missing, offline, or failed disks and volumes. Try: select disk X, online disk to bring disks online.

fixSteps.title: Import foreign disks
fixSteps.instruction: If disks show as Foreign in diskpart, select the disk and run: import. This re-registers the dynamic disk configuration.

fixSteps.title: Convert to basic disks
fixSteps.instruction: If dynamic disk configuration is unrecoverable, back up data, then convert to basic disks: select disk X, convert basic. Warning: this destroys all data on dynamic volumes.

whenToCallPro: If data on dynamic volumes is important and the configuration is corrupted, a professional can use specialized tools to recover data before rebuilding the disk configuration.

faq.q: What are dynamic disks?
faq.a: Dynamic disks are a Windows disk management feature that supports spanned, striped, mirrored, and RAID-5 volumes. They use a special database to track volume configuration.

faq.q: Should I use dynamic disks?
faq.a: For most users, basic disks with GPT partitions are recommended. Dynamic disks add complexity. Use Storage Spaces or hardware RAID for redundancy instead.

faq.q: Can I recover data from a failed dynamic disk?
faq.a: Often yes. Professional data recovery services can usually recover data from failed dynamic disk configurations, even when Windows cannot mount the volumes.

title: Kernel WMI Internal Error
shortDescription: An internal error occurred in the Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) kernel component.
fullDescription:
This BSOD occurs when the kernel-mode WMI (Windows Management Instrumentation) subsystem encounters an internal consistency error. WMI is used extensively by Windows and applications for system management, monitoring, and event notification.

The error can be triggered by corrupted WMI repository, buggy WMI providers (often from third-party management software), or driver issues that corrupt WMI data structures.

Resolution typically involves rebuilding the WMI repository or updating the WMI provider driver.
symptoms: Blue screen with KERNEL_WMI_INTERNAL
WMI queries fail before crash
System management tools stop working
Event log shows WMI errors
Performance monitoring tools crash

fixSteps.title: Rebuild WMI repository
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt. Stop WMI: net stop winmgmt. Rebuild: winmgmt /resetrepository. Restart: net start winmgmt.

fixSteps.title: Check WMI consistency
fixSteps.instruction: Run: winmgmt /verifyrepository. If it reports inconsistency, run: winmgmt /salvagerepository to attempt repair.

fixSteps.title: Remove third-party management software
fixSteps.instruction: Temporarily uninstall system management, monitoring, or inventory software that registers WMI providers. Test if stability improves.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Run DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth followed by sfc /scannow to repair any corrupted system files affecting WMI.

whenToCallPro: If WMI repository rebuilds do not resolve the issue and the error occurs on a production server, a professional sysadmin should investigate WMI provider conflicts.

faq.q: What is WMI?
faq.a: WMI (Windows Management Instrumentation) is a Windows infrastructure for managing data and operations. It is used by many system tools, scripts, and monitoring software.

faq.q: Will rebuilding WMI lose settings?
faq.a: Resetting the repository rebuilds it to default. Third-party software may need to re-register their WMI providers. System WMI providers rebuild automatically.

faq.q: Is this common on servers?
faq.a: It is more common on servers running management agents, SCCM clients, or monitoring software that heavily use WMI.

title: Win32k Critical Failure
shortDescription: The Win32k display subsystem encountered a critical failure.
fullDescription:
The WIN32K_CRITICAL_FAILURE BSOD indicates that the Win32k kernel subsystem, which handles the Windows graphical user interface and display management, encountered an unrecoverable error. Win32k.sys manages windows, display output, input devices, and the graphical environment.

This error is commonly caused by display driver issues, corrupted Windows graphical components, or conflicts between multiple display drivers. It can also occur during Remote Desktop sessions.

Resolving this typically requires clean display driver installation and repairing Windows system files.
symptoms: Blue screen with WIN32K_CRITICAL_FAILURE
Screen goes black or garbled before crash
Crash during graphical operations (video, games, desktop composition)
Error after display driver update
Crash during Remote Desktop session

fixSteps.title: Boot into Safe Mode
fixSteps.instruction: Restart and boot into Safe Mode (minimal display driver). If system is stable in Safe Mode, the display driver is likely the cause.

fixSteps.title: Clean install display drivers
fixSteps.instruction: In Safe Mode, run DDU to completely remove display drivers. Reboot and install the latest stable driver from the GPU manufacturer.

fixSteps.title: Run SFC and DISM
fixSteps.instruction: Open elevated Command Prompt: DISM /Online /Cleanup-Image /RestoreHealth then sfc /scannow to repair Win32k and related system files.

fixSteps.title: Disable hardware acceleration
fixSteps.instruction: In Windows Settings > Display > Graphics, set hardware-accelerated GPU scheduling to Off. Also disable hardware acceleration in applications that trigger the crash.

whenToCallPro: If clean driver installation and system file repair do not resolve the issue, the GPU hardware may be failing and professional diagnosis is needed.

faq.q: What is Win32k?
